failure_indicators: No addresses are deleted after the victim visits the payload. Pivot: Consider increasing the range of `addressId` if the initial range was too small. If still unsuccessful, the `addressId` may be protected by other means or the exploit is not feasible.
The browser becomes unresponsive due to excessive requests. Pivot: Implement delays between requests in the JavaScript POC or send requests sequentially rather than in a rapid loop.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can cause a logged-in user to involuntarily delete their shipping addresses from their account, leading to inconvenience and potential disruption.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for CSRF protection on any state-changing operations, especially those involving unique, user-specific identifiers, and consider brute-forcing numeric IDs when CSRF protection is absent.

meta.title: How I made 1000$ with AT&T Bug Bounty(H1)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user's account settings can be modified via a POST request without adequate CSRF protection.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session in one browser.
Victim must have an authenticated session in another browser.
applicability_signals: The existence of an account editing endpoint (e.g., /customer/account/editPost/).
The form submission does not contain a unique, session-specific anti-CSRF token.
skip_if: The account edit POST request returns a CSRF token validation error.
The application enforces SameSite cookies that prevent cross-origin POST requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to a victim account on https://www.att.com.mx/tienda/customer/account/editPost/ using a browser.
Step 2: Observe the HTTP POST request when submitting account changes using a web proxy (e.g., Burp Suite). Look for the presence of an anti-CSRF token in the request parameters or headers.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use Burp Suite's 'Engagement tools' -> 'Generate PoC request' on the intercepted POST request to an account edit endpoint.
Step 2: Save the generated HTML PoC file (e.g., csrf.htm).
Step 3: Modify the 'email' or 'password' fields within the saved HTML PoC to the attacker's desired values.
Step 4: Instruct the victim to open the crafted HTML file (e.g., csrf.htm) in their browser while they are logged into their AT&T account.
Step 5: The victim's browser will automatically submit the modified account details to the AT&T server.
Step 6: Verify the account takeover by checking the victim's account details in a separate browser session.
payloads_or_patterns:
<form action="https://www.att.com.mx/tienda/customer/account/editPost/" method="POST">
  <input type="hidden" name="email" value="attacker-email@example.com" />
  <input type="hidden" name="form_key" value="EXPECTED_FORM_KEY_IF_PRESENT" />
  <input type="submit" value="Submit" />
</form>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and generate PoC, modify request parameters.
success_indicators: Successful modification of the victim's account email or password as confirmed by logging into the victim's account with the new credentials.
Response from the server indicating a successful account update (e.g., 200 OK with a success message).
failure_indicators: The application returns an error indicating an invalid CSRF token (e.g., 403 Forbidden or a specific error message). Pivot: Investigate other state-changing actions for CSRF vulnerabilities or look for other vulnerability types.
The PoC request does not result in any account changes and returns an error or redirects to a login page.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of the victim's AT&T account, allowing the attacker to change credentials, access personal information, or perform unauthorized actions on behalf of the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement and validate anti-CSRF tokens on all state-changing requests, especially those that modify user account details.

meta.title: GraphQL Introspection leads to Sensitive Data Disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: The target application uses GraphQL and exposes a /graphql endpoint to authenticated users. GraphQL introspection should not be disabled.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session with the target application.
applicability_signals: GraphQL endpoint is discoverable (e.g., '/infosec/graphql').
Authenticated requests to the GraphQL endpoint return responses containing GraphQL schema information or types like '__typename'.
skip_if: GraphQL introspection is explicitly disabled or returns errors for introspection queries.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the GraphQL endpoint. Look for paths like '/graphql', '/api/graphql', or any custom GraphQL endpoint. Fingerprint by sending requests and observing responses for GraphQL-specific patterns.
Step 2: Authenticate to the target application and capture a GraphQL request (e.g., during profile update) using Burp Suite. Observe the request and response for GraphQL schema details or types.
attack_steps: Step 1: Capture an authenticated GraphQL request (e.g., from Profile Update page) in Burp Suite.
Step 2: Send the captured request to Burp Repeater.
Step 3: In Burp GraphQL extension or Repeater, attempt to enumerate information by modifying queries or looking for errors. The writeup mentions finding 'userHash' by trying to enumerate from errors.
Step 4: Once potential fields are identified (e.g., 'userHash'), try to include them in introspection queries.
Step 5: Construct and execute the GraphQL Introspection Query.
Step 6: If the introspection query fails with an 'operationName' error, change the 'operationName' to 'IntrospectionQuery'.
Step 7: Execute the modified introspection query and analyze the response for the full GraphQL schema, which may include sensitive data fields.
payloads_or_patterns: query IntrospectionQuery { __schema { queryType { name } mutationType { name } subscriptionType { name } types { ...FullType } directives { name description args { ...InputValue } onOperation onFragment onField } } } fragment FullType on __Type { kind name description fields(includeDeprecated: true) { name description args { ...InputValue } type { ...TypeRef } isDeprecated deprecationReason } inputFields { ...InputValue } interfaces { ...TypeRef } enumValues(includeDeprecated: true) { name description isDeprecated deprecationReason } possibleTypes { ...TypeRef } } fragment InputValue on __InputValue { name description type { ...TypeRef } defaultValue } fragment TypeRef on __Type { kind name ofType { kind name ofType { kind name ofType { kind name } } } }
Modified Introspection Query with correct operationName: operationName: IntrospectionQuery
Example of finding userHash: query { __typename userHash }
tools_recommended: Burp Suite
Burp Suite GraphQL Raider extension
Burp Suite JSON Beautifier extension
success_indicators: Successful execution of the Introspection Query returns the complete GraphQL schema.
The returned schema contains details about types, fields, and potentially sensitive data fields that were not intended to be public.
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or similar errors when executing introspection queries without the correct operation name.
500 Internal Server Errors indicating a problem with the GraphQL server.
GraphQL responses that do not contain any schema information, suggesting introspection might be disabled or limited.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of the entire GraphQL schema, potentially revealing sensitive data fields, internal types, and query/mutation structures that can be leveraged for further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test GraphQL endpoints for introspection queries, especially when authenticated. Ensure introspection is disabled or restricted in production environments.
